entry.title: Meal and a Drink
entry.content: Today I wanted to drink because I was having a nice meal and I wanted something to drink with it, because the very act of consuming nourishment has become inexorably linked with imbibing alcohol. Food for the soul, at the cost of the body. Like any addiction it worms its way into your habits, into necessary actions. It becomes a prerequisite until you can’t even think to do those activities without it. Absent mindedly holding the drink menu and dropping it like an orphan source of radiation. As if you’re holding a bomb that could blow your life apart. That’s how it starts, just a beer with a meal, then two then three. Then you drink before a meal, and after, and at any time of the day, holding to whatever arbitrary rules you have. No spirits with a meal, no alcohol before noon, all ways of lawyering away your own alcoholism. You could never be an alcoholic; it’s just called being cultured. It’s just a wine pairing with some good meat; it helps bring out the flavour. As you pickle your own organs day after day imbibing the good stuff. A flambeed dessert tastes so good, why wouldn’t a human body soaked in alcohol for years taste any different. A richer flavour of bleeding wounds. In France they have a dish, a whole baby bird, drowned in alcohol and cooked. It’s customarily eaten under a tablecloth to shield yourself from God’s judgement as you eat something unholy. I am the baby bird drowning, I am the patron eating, I am God’s eyes, I am a trinity eating my own soul, damning myself to hell, dissolving my consciousness into sweet nothing. It’s so cultured, so new and futuristic, it’s so delicious. As glug after glug of fine wine goes into me, I will drown soon. Unless I stop pouring. That is why I won’t drink.

entry.title: Movie
entry.content:
Today I wanted to drink because I watched a movie. It was about a guy who gambled like me, not with chips, not for money, well not directly for money. I gamble with years of my life. He did too. Gambling with his future time and time again, going into the pulpit. Humiliating himself once, all for money. He was bent over and hit with a paddle. He said he would do anything. I would do anything; I’ve been hit with that paddle so many times. Over and over that paddle. Then at the climax of the final act he’s faced with a final humiliation, which he avoids, he’s able to assert himself. In the end of the film, he’s able to accept his life, and find a happy ending that was laid out in front of him. A happy ending I would kill for. A happy ending I don’t get the option of. I want to gamble and not have red ink fill my mind. It’s selfish and childish. I am selfish. I am childish. I’m stupid and that’s why I drink. Burning away the losses. Letting the ink bleed away. I just want a chance to be happy.

Maybe if I was more modest if I found simpler goals. If I wasn’t like this. If I roped. If I roped if I had managed to rope, not that pathetic attempt, the one that was just for attention. Not that one. Maybe then I could have been able to have my gambling pay off.

He’s just a film character. I’m a real person. It was all just an act. Though every day I’ve always been looking. People with friends, with good stories to fill their minds, friends to call on who are close to them. I’ve always been watching, watching other people. I never asked to have a family like this. I never asked to go to a school full of people who hated me.

I just wanted some of their warmth. Sat out in the cold looking at fires. I never asked for my ex. I clung to them because at least another human body at least that’s warmth. At least they’re warm. They can stab me, they can shout at me just give me warmth. I’ve only ever known being hit, when I go for warmth. The two are linked.

Warmth and shouting, loud voices and warmth. Alcohol makes me feel warm too. I’ll always have my belly trailing after me begging for another drink. One shot, two shots, three shots, four shots, some beer, a blood alcohol to entitle me to join other people sitting around their fire. To let them share their warmth with me for a little while. That’s why I wanted to drink. That’s why I won’t drink. To keep the warmth, I have. That’s why I won’t drink.

entry.title: Non-Essential Machinery
entry.content:
The courts’ distinction between essential and non-essential machinery for resolving contractual uncertainties is rather arbitrary. Discuss. Contract law acts to “provide individuals with facilities for realizing their wishes,” for an element of that law to be arbitrary it needs to grant some wishes but not others, with no good reason distinguishing them. This essay will evaluate arbitrariness by examining two principal cases, evaluating if the rules within them arose arbitrarily without prior precedent, and if they broke from prior precedent they did so with no justification, and if the rules created are applied inconsistently. The principal cases to be dealt with are Sudbrook v. Eggleton (Sudbrook) and Gillatt v. Sky Television (Gillatt). In Sudbrook the machinery was held to be non-essential and when it broke down the contract could be salvaged. In Gillatt that was not the case and the machinery was held to be essential. Overall, the rule in Sudbrook is not arbitrary despite overturning a ‘long line of authorities,’ it did so with clear justification. Gillatt is also not arbitrary since it gives clear justification to distinguish it from Sudbrook.

Sudbrook, decided in 1982 represents a case that in many ways departs from long established legal principles and in that sense, it could be seen has having arisen arbitrarily. The machinery was held non-essential at the first instance, being overturned on appeal through application of Milnes v. Gery (Milnes). Milnes bears a strong factual resemblance to Sudbrook, both cases concerned a fair price to be determined by specified contractual machinery which broke down due to non-performance by one of the parties. In Milnes the court held in the words of Grant MR “no price having ever been fixed in that mode, the parties have not agreed upon any price.” That the lack of agreement in the specified machinery rendered the contract too uncertain to be enforceable, the machinery was too essential to be severed from the contract. Without a decided price then it could be said the price was “quasi nullo pretio statuto,” as though there was no set price. With no price and price being an essential part of contract, there was no contract to enforce. This was a clear rule.

Milnes would go on to be applied in other cases with a strong factual resemblance, in particular Vickers v. Vickers and Hart v. Hart. Though it was contradicted at points for example in Gregory v. Mighell concluded 4 years after Milnes. That being said in Sudbrook reference was made by Lord Diplock to the notion that this “long and consistent line of authorities [was overruled],” in Sudbrook. This reference makes clear that Sudbrook and the rule that if machinery breaks down it can be substituted for a ‘fair price,’ so as to give intention to the parties, arose contrary to prior precedent regarding the issue despite going against prior precedent and thus opening the door to arbitrariness. That being said perhaps Sudbrook represents bringing the law on non-essential machinery in line with wider principles of English contract law. Indeed, Sudbrook is an articulation of the modern principles of English contract law with regard to bargains. Sudbrook ultimately held that while the court could not substitute its own machinery, the machinery was in effect to find a fair price, and demonstrated the objective intent of the parties to sell at a fair price. Therefore, the courts should give effect to those wishes and find a fair price. Sudbrook merely brought the rules concerning non-essential machinery in line with the rules on non- essential contract terms at large, as found in Nicolene v. Simmonds, Bear Stearns Bank plc. v. Forum Global Equity ltd. and Hillias v. Arcos in that sense Sudbrook cannot be said to have arisen arbitrarily. It arose for good reason out of the ambiguities in Milnes. It represents a consolidation of the principle of English courts articulated by Lord Wright in Hillas v. Arcos, ‘verba ita sunt intelligenda ut res magis valeat quam perat.’ Words are to be interpreted so as to give effect to the subject matter rather than to defeat it. In this sense Sudbrook may not have arisen arbitrarily and merely brought the area in line with wider developments regarding uncertainty in English contract law at the time. However, that the Lords in Sudbrook made reference to the ‘long line of authorities,’ it is clearer that the principle in Sudbrook did buck prior precedent and was arbitrary as a result. However, while the rule arose arbitrarily, perhaps it was done for good reason, Lazarides saying in reference to Sudbrook, “that a rough and ready agreement to sell at a fair price would be enforceable, but that a well drafted clause which specified an agreed method for arriving at the contract price would not be.” This demonstrates that the rule in Milnes served to undermine well drafted bargains, while the courts gave effect to less carefully drafted bargains. Despite the fact that a more carefully drafted bargain demonstrates objectively more intent to be bound. Equally it ignores the underlying intent that a ‘well drafted clause,’ is first and foremost intended to find a fair and equal price for both parties. As such the rule in Sudbrook worked to give effect to this intention, holding that the specific machinery was non-essential and a fair price was the true intent. The court acted to become the defender of bargains.

That being said, the argument made by Lazarides, regarding the inconsistencies between a “well drafted clause [with] an agreed method for arriving at a contract price,” and a “rough and ready agreement to sell at a fair price,” disregards the notion that a well drafted clause with specific machinery in mind is the objective intention of the parties. Parties may draft a clause carefully and freely with the intention that the machinery be essential. Freedom of contract, and specifically term-freedom the notion that “the free area within which the parties are permitted, in principle, to set their own terms should be maximised. ” In this sense Sudbrook with its emphasis on the true intention set aside from the terms undermines the ability of parties to ‘set their own terms.’ In Sudbrook the court effectively created their own terms out of the apparent intention of the parties. This could easily be seen as arbitrary as any ‘well drafted clause,’ could be set aside if the court sees it as being seemingly contrary to the court breathed true intentions of the parties. If the true intention of the parties was to arrive at a fair price and the machinery was just a term to make real that intention, then the parties should have incorporated their intention as a term in the contract. The term at issue in the contract does not include any reference to a fair and reasonable price and such a term could only ever be implied and potentially arbitrary in the sense of undermining the certainty of contract.

Yet such a black-letter interpretation of term-freedom would expose the parties to considerable risk and ignore the reliance placed on agreements between the parties. The extensive drafting of the clause created clear intention according to Lord Diplock “they must have intended to create legal rights to have those provisions acted on by both parties and not flouted by either party at his own sweet will.

” As such the extensive drafting created a clear intention at the time for the parties to be bound. This was contradicted later by the refusal to appoint a valuer but the intention at the moment of offer and acceptance is what matters. As such the court was giving effect to the intention at the parties at the time of contract, and reading the intention from the term. The machinery had one intention and the inclusion of a minimum price demonstrated this intent further still, even if no term was incorporated about a fair price. In that sense the term could be seen as not being arbitrary. Overall, while arising arbitrarily the distinction between essential and non-essential machinery exists for good reason. It enables the courts to act to protect bargains as well as giving effect to the objective intention of the parties at the time of entering into a contract. The next element of arbitrariness to be dealt with concerns inconsistent application. The decisions in Sudbrook and Gillatt need to be reconciled. Failure to do so would demonstrate inconsistent application and therefore arbitrariness. Gillatt determined that a chartered accountant was essential to the contract. In Sudbrook it concerned a valuer. This may seem arbitrary but it is an important distinction. In Sudbrook this eventuality is mentioned directly with Lord Diplock saying in Sudbrook “[where a] price [is] fixed by a named individual applying such subjective standards as he personally thinks fit, and that individual, […] refuses to fix the price or is unable through death or disability to do so, the contract of sale is thereupon determined by frustration.” While the term at issue in Gillatt, Clause 6.1, does not give a name it does give a clear occupation “an independent chartered accountant.” Independent chartered accountants do not necessarily arrive at a fair price but use their own specific expertise to arrive at a valuation. Therefore, the objective intention is not to use the accountant as the machinery to arrive at a fair price but to use the accountant’s skills to find what the price ought to be. This may appear to be a very slight distinction but it can be distinguished and it gives effect to term-freedom within contract. The differing methods used by different accountants render the term closer to a May and Butcher v. R, ‘price to be determined from time to time,’ as well. As such the lack of a certain fair price to find independently of the machinery makes Gillatt different from Sudbrook and a crucial refinement of the rule in Sudbrook.

That being said it could be argued that it is an arbitrary distinction without a place in contract law. Could it not be argued that the clause in Gillatt was even more “well drafted,” and Sudbrook was more of a “rough and ready agreement,” and as such the courts were essentially reverting the rule in Sudbrook to prioritize upholding vaguer agreements while letting well drafted ones fail. This may seem the case on first assessment but through the clearer drafting the term becomes essential through application of the doctrine of term- freedom. By clarifying their wishes even more clearly yet not incorporate a term specifying their intent to find a fair price they demonstrated they intended to be bound by that essential term rather than using the term as merely a mechanism to give effect to their intentions. As such Gillatt does not represent an inconsistency with Sudbrook and it refines the rule to give effect to wider principles in English contract law. Though again we must justify term-freedom on its own merits. After all, would it not be arbitrary to hew to principle and not question the validity of said principle. With regards to contract law, “Frequently one party has little effective choice in the matter at all, and neither reads nor understands, nor in any real sense agrees to the terms contained in such standard documents.

” As such this lack of choice could undermine term-freedom. However with both Sudbrook and Gillatt this is not operant as all parties are commercial enterprises and thus they are endowed with ‘effective,’ choice. Ultimately, while the rule in Sudbrook does overturn a long line of prior authorities and in doing so arise arbitrarily, it does so for good reason, to give effect to the intention of the parties when contracting, and it is not applied inconsistently and arbitrarily, with Gillatt giving clearer rules and clarifying Sudbrook. On this application it is clear that the distinction is not rather arbitrary, but grounded in clear principles of English contract law, and applied clearly.

entry.title: An Account of State Power
entry.content:
It is beyond easy for those of us with as little contact with the state as possible to lionize it. When the arm of the state reaches but into your pocket to take the sum of taxes owed and all other mechanisms obscured or valorised, it becomes easy to support the state as it exists. To be in such a state is to be an emancipated adult, earning one’s way and living freely and independently. The state does not bind you anymore than you should like with the exception of a few ground rules which you might well want to keep yourself anyway, rules which maintain the relationship and maintain lines of communication.

The state is an animal in the dark and the more you feel the more you begin to recognize its true shape. Fingers that reach into your wallet, arms that bind, a head that thinks, eyes that see. It is only through contact with the state that you recognize what it truly is. It is being fashioned more and more into a parent. Into a figure of control for those of us who are most under its thumb.

I live with the state breathing down my neck, I live with several states binding me. Preventing me from achieving my will. It could be said that the state is an alignment of wills collectively deciding to chart a national course. I am mostly not swayed by such arguments. The state is a beast far bigger than its constituent parts. That is what gives it such tremendous power and power for good. This is not an anarchist manifesto it is appealing to the current role of the state and the role it should have. We have fashioned it into a parent. It is the collective failure of the social order to create a parental state.

States originated as people as small tribes, small groups of hunter gatherers, then walled farming compounds and villages. The stronger state always won and that came with the ability to organize. Eventually the farmers work in service of a king who commands an army who can use the material tyranny of violence to exert power. Power is created two principle ways, by the tyranny of material reality manifest through violence, and through the policeman in one’s own head to manipulate the emotional reality felt by one’s subjects.

Power has always existed in a mixture of the two. It is forcing people with violence to act as you so wish, or making them act as you so wish by making them believe doing so would be the best course of action. Violence is always the least preferrable option in this case. For one violence can operate much better as a threat. The application of violence is best used to restrain and to radically prevent one from doing as one wants, it is little use in a positive application and the threat of violence which is in many ways part of the policeman in one’s own head that is a more effective means of exerting power.

In our larval tribal stage as a species organization was through familial bonds largely. Violence existed of course, cheddar man did not have a cave full of butcher marked human bones for nothing after all. Violence was a tool for acquiring what you want and preventing negative action by those who would obstruct you. However to create something that you wanted the other means was far more important. You needed to cultivate that internal policeman to organize. Indeed every person doing violence in the name of another, beyond just petty theft is doing so at the behest of that policeman in their own head. It is not imposed directly. It is accepted by them for whatever reason.

State power is the growth of largely this power. The creation of community relies on this power and the state as a gigantic imagined community represents the greatest innovation in the effective utilization of this power to its greatest extent. State power has become so natural that criminalization is effectively treated as a statement of morality. Though we cannot ever detect immorality waves when putting bodies in the town water supply, the illegality of something is treated as the same as an immorality particle given off with every body that is placed in that reservoir.

Morality and law and the state and the social order are such a tightly bound cluster of concepts that each one acts to re-enforce the other. The rule of law is an admission by the state that it sees application of the law as the most expedient means of using its own power. Through an appeal to non-arbitrary application of the rules of society and an appeal to justice it becomes much easier for the policeman in one’s own head to acquiesce. The greater the distance you have between the real fingers of the state the easier this façade is to uphold.

Of course, the law is far from the only means of exercising state power and lionizing the role of the rule of law is nothing more than a propaganda tactic. Law has left the building insofar as it ever was here in human history. Rules were made through custom and custom was given effect by law. Law is staffed by lawyers who are in themselves people with their own biases and decision-making. There is also no escape through appealing to the reverse to throw oneself into the pool of precedent and statutes and let yourself be carried by the current of history. To do so would be to forfeit legal decision-making to the biases of those who came before you. To rely on the text of a statute is to forfeit yourself to the biases of those who composed those words. Law is arbitrary it can only ever be arbitrary just as any other human system is. There is no justice particle given off when the judge reads an appropriately just sentence.

As justice is meaningless there is nothing but the assemblage of sounds conveyed through the air in rooms well lit filled with electricity from far off power plants, assembled by people to manipulate the forces of nature themselves, the sounds are made by flaps in the back of your mouth, air breathed into the room in line with pulsing electricity in your mind. It is all contingent and it always only was. All the law can do is appeal to consistency. This is what happened before and thus so on and so forth until we are not animated meat but merely a curious assemblage of calcium. Because consistency mirrors how we perceive the natural world. Seasons come and go the sun rises and sets. We pay no mind to the reality of why such things occur but merely the pattern and shape of them. They occur regularly enough ergo they will always occur and occur in ways that rhyme nicely with each other.

Law and justice are not linked. The charge that rule of law is not arbitrary is on the face of it incorrect. Law is subordinate to the social order at large. It imposes some rules yes on contracts, on law, it gives effect to things it allows individuals to use state power to achieve as they wish. Of course nobody is under any obligation to comply with the courts. It is through that policeman that they do either mental or through physical violence, yet every second you are not being cuffed and manhandled or being forced to move it is up to you. State power is a powerful body that may tear lives apart by making any other option unconscionable and even through applying violence. It gives structure, but the more we feed the beast the stronger its muscles become and that musculature can rip us limb from limb. It diffuses social responsibility and harms us all collectively. The state is an abusive parent and the more power we give it, the younger we are by comparison. The more vulnerable the person the more the tendrils of the state wrap around us. Yet the state is such a natural existence that it is treated as an axiom by itself. So naturally has it legitimated itself that state power could be counted among the forces of gravity and electromagnetism as the defining aspects of material reality for a human being in the 21st century.

We nominally have control of the state through elections but do most people even acknowledge what is spat out by the state? They vote for the guy with the flashiest smile on the ad. The guy their friends are voting for. They vote to prop up their own identity, I am such and such a person, and as such and such a person I will vote for so and so, and so on. Or not vote at all in some cases. The individual identity of someone is far more defining as a feature for who they’ll vote for than anything else. That makes sense to someone too. In the good old days of tribes and such, the barbarous times those were, we knew our leaders and we were in a sense legislators within the tribe, but people rose to the top. No organization is in effect organization by those who wish to take it the most. Other people acquiesce to those others and so in voting we acquiesce to those who are seeking control. We submit to a leader through voting. This is the opposite to what we are told, rule for and by the people, our leaders should rule in trust for us. How can one individual or even a group of individuals even contend with the state? They cannot.

We submit to the state in the hopes that we will not be beaten by its belt, we hope that it will beat someone else with its belt and then share whatever spoils gained in the act. Sometimes people want to see others beat with a belt. The faceless gratuitous pain of watching someone else’s life burn by itself. Though such animals are in the minority. Most see life as zero sum, nothing can be gained without another losing. Voting can be tremendously effective in changing something but the disrupted line between voters and politicians effectively mean that even in a democracy there is miscommunication and missed opportunities. Once endowed with power politicians act however they choose and we submit to them we give them that power to do as they please we provide them the legitimacy and as such state action is more easily swallowed by us. We would not accept individuals acting under their own authority as the state does, and the meaningful difference is the kill chain which leads to us at the top. We are at the top logically all political decisions can be traced to us and we are responsible for it, but there is a gap between us and who we vote for. It is up to us to hold them to account and prevent their excesses but as politics is an expression of individual identity that ceases to count for anything.

entry.title: Ministry of Event Management
entry.content:
Chapter 1: Dissection

She hummed the words softly to herself. This is the day your life will surely change. She fell into the decayed chair. A heart of wood and red carpet, marble floors, and the rotting grandeur of a long-dead empire. That was Whitehall, the office Whiskey found herself in. The department for the North, Ministry of Event Management, Section 5, Artefacts. It was a government position. Blood pumping through the heart nourishing its gnarled frame. She was dressed in the best clothes she owned, an old white shirt made for a man from a charity shop. Second hand slacks that were too small for her. An oversized cardigan from her ex. There were others stuck in the same temporal vortex. Waiting for their turn. One by one. Some were dressed better, some worse. Some with stains. Some with more egregiously ill-fitting clothes. She was in the bottom quartile of style but that was not somewhere she was unfamiliar with. This was all she had anyway. That and the memories that hold her life together like glue.

They were all pushed deep into the bowels of the building to begin the digestive process. Applicant by applicant chewed through. There was no new infusion as the sun started to hang down in the sky. There was a clear divide with the alts like her called out by ID numbers, and the others being called out by names. Still, it gave her a kick seeing normies forced to apply for the same dismal employment that she did, chasing that office job, those comfortable hours. That thought chased by a worry, that she would be passed over. She could not afford the luxury of failure. At least being called a number was a kind of neutrality and clarity. There were worst things she could be called by them.

Her ears heard her number before she did, and had already called up her legs into motion. She stood up without even realizing it. She looked for the person who said her name. Head moving, jerking around, ears atop her head straining, black tail straight behind her. It took the person moving her hand for her to see. All that time spent staring at the passage every other applicant headed down, and not looking for where she needed to go. Placing herself in the invisible pre-screening pecking order.

She headed down the passage through some stairs and past a weary looking security guard who did not spare her even a nod. She went upstairs and down stairs and, she was already six feet under. Eventually reached a chair outside a room with a low ceiling. The ornate woodwork carpentry had become more functional as she descended deeper but the marble floors and ornate carpets remained. The lanyard wearing woman who guided her then went into the room behind her after gesturing towards the folding black metal chair. Whiskey sat down feeling the cold of the chair. She had no idea of what was in the room behind her, who her appraisers would be. If they would think she was a pleasing pound of flesh. Her feet tapped away. She tried to stay nervous. She knew that this job was more for her than for anyone else. They did not put in the effort; they did not know the pain that she did. They had other chances, other places to go.

“Hi.” A voice called out cheerfully from behind her. She stood up on impulse again without thinking then awkwardly second-guessed herself. “Oh, I’m just wondering if I could take a quick look at your papers if that’s alright.” She was a little older than her using a casual tone of someone embarrassed about their own age and authority. The woman’s surroundings made it stand out even more.

Whiskey took out her government ID, instead of a name she had a designation W8194-HB, it had been branded into her memory through repetition just as it had been branded into her as what she was to be called by. Not Whiskey. Not by the old name that still haunted her. Not by the impolite language reserved for her kind. The neutral clarity of a number. “Here you go madam, Miss.” She instantly felt awkwardly as the word madam left her lips. Too formal for that woman. Whiskey awkwardly started stretching the back of her neck.

“All in order.” The woman said scanning it, her eyes clearly lingered on the M under gender. Even when removed from personhood, the specter of legal masculinity still haunted her. Whiskey still had not been given a name for her. Shared namelessness, at least to Whiskey. “Please take a seat, we will be with you shortly.”

Whiskey slumped in the chair; she should have known that she was not to stand up. She was tapping her feet but this time more sporadically. The anxious rhythm attracting a glare from the security guard. She ceased her movement. Her hands formed into fists. Her mind kept on reaching to find some reason for why she felt so tense. This should be nothing, this was her element. She knew all about it. She knew everything she had to. She was steel, she was not wearing makeup, nothing expensive, she was made of stronger stuff than that woman.

The door opened again and again she stood up, this time sure that she was being called in. It was a man this time, bald, bespectacled, and bearded. He looked at her for a moment before passing by and down one of the winding venous corridors. She slumped back down again. Off balance and wheeling from self-imposed embarrassment. The man came back in a few minutes later. She was still waiting. She traced the carpet trying to distract herself and this time succeeding. The beige color of the carpet, and crème-colored walls, and the little gold cresting wave as the carpet met the blue carpeted stairs.

The door opened again. She stood up again. The woman was there now.

“Please come in.” She said.

This time instead of stumbling over titles she just nodded and went in. The man was bald with a grey neatly trimmed beard.

“Hello. Welcome.” He said with a kind of warmth. “Should we do introductions or?” He looked over to her, she gave him a look. He cleared his throat. “So you are a alt, sorry an altered human correct? You mentioned in your letter that you first had contact with an artefact at age 14, would you be able to tell me about that then. About your experience with resonance artefacts in general. Your legal sex it was male correct?” His eyes burned into her like a magnifying glass. She was just an ant. His voice stayed flat.

“Yes sir.” She paused. “Yes sir I have been altered.” She left the rest of it unaddressed. “The first time I saw an artefact was when I was 14, it was during the event. There was all this noise everywhere. It was the first time I ever saw a window break. First time I saw the walls come down. I was afraid, but. I saw it. It was a different sound from the rumbling outside. A high-pitched crackle. I can never forget all the colors inside of it. I reached my hand out to touch it. The crackling got louder and louder behind my ears. I can’t really remember the rest after that, but the artefact was contained.”

“I see. That’s hardly an atypical experience. You know we do get a lot of applications from altered individuals, and often they have stories like yours but not always. Go on tell me have you even ever felt the resonance yourself.”

“Sir?”

“Like I said go on.”

“Well sir, this was before I was altered, but after that event I developed a strong interest in artefacts, and that led me to visiting the zone after the containment wind down. It was not exactly a sanctioned visit, but it was the second time I encountered an artefact.”

“Which zone, black? Red? Amber?”

“Red zone sir. It was when there was a wind-down of operations around Bristol.”

“Go on.”

“Yes sir, I encountered an artefact. It was blue and it had a different kind of crackle. I heard it without seeing it initially. The sound got louder and louder and things started to, well spin sir. I felt the sound crawling into me, into my spine. The second stretched out into an endless moment that drowned me backwards. That was when I was altered.”

“I see, so a second occurrence, how many times have you visited the zone?”

“Five, no, six times in all sir.”

“All red?”

“Black once, red three times, amber twice.” She paused. “Sir.”

“Quite well travelled then, so you’re familiar with the different kinds of things we tend to find in each part of the zone?”

“Yes sir.”

“Have any blessings in you?”

“Yes sir. Ever since I was altered, I never a final end.”

“You mean to tell me you cannot die.”

“No sir.”

“Have you tried?”

“Yes sir.”

The woman stood up to interrupt. “Could you perhaps show us?”

“Helen, we can’t ask her, we can’t ask him to do something like that.” The bald man took back control.

“Hand me a gun.” Whiskey said.

“This is not the time or place for that, I am quite sure you are genuine.” He paused. “It is not exactly outside the remit of what could be expected from a blessing albeit a little more potent and immediately useful than we usually see. That being said, I must ask, does it hurt?”

“Yes sir.”

“Yes sir?”

“More than you’ll ever know sir.”

The interview carried on mentioning details about the job but those words hung as a fog in the air. At the end handshakes were exchanged and so the long wait would begin she thought. She left the office and was directed back through the passageways by the weary security guard. The lobby had mostly emptied out and all that was left was functionaries busy with their own jobs. She signed out at the book before leaving the place behind her. It was going to be a long and for her quite pricey bus ride back to the Vauxhall apartment of her ex who she was still living with. She hammered her body into the shape of every step forward. Until a few months ago they were so happy then it all came down. It was lucky that she was allowed to stay in the first place, the ex’s small benevolence. Those two years. She did not want to return but she had nowhere else to go. As she crossed the threshold, she announced her presence before settling in her nest. It was a black couch in the living room with a grey blanket and a couple of couch cushions that had become pillows.

Her ex was in the office down near the bedroom and deeper into the apartment. The door was closed and locked as it always was. She liked to tell herself it was because her ex was busy doing something sensitive, the ex was an American associated with the ever-sprawling increasing influence of that government. Always tapping away at the ex’s hybrid working job. The kind of job that would always remain under lock and key. In her heart she knew however that the locked door was not because of the ex’s work. It was not because Whiskey could not face what lay within, but because what lay within could not face her. The smothering scummy presence she was. Too clingy, too worthless. Slag on a scrapheap. She rested for a moment on the couch for a minute, she considered reheating some dinner for herself but she did not feel hungry. She felt sick. Her kidneys hurt her body ached. She reheated some for the occupant beyond the door, leaving it outside the door and then settling in for bed. Trying to shut out the smell before it made her vomit.

Sleep did not come easy. Her leg jostled for attention. With her stomach. A live wire stinging her forcing her to move it every few minutes. It carried on. She thought about the man and his expressions as he dug around in her answers. She told herself that worrying was like chewing gum to solve an equation but it did not stop her worries. It never did. Eventually she fell asleep.

She woke up to the ex making coffee. She feigned sleeping until the door was closed and the ex was on the way to the embassy complex. Whiskey put on the TV and let the news wash over her. The weather was going to be rainy all day, the perfect day to stay in and watch TV and wait to see if any job offers came in the post. She had to get it she could not avoid getting it. If she was denied she would have to live longer here, on this couch. She could not go North her family did not want to see her and she did not want to see them and aside from this couch she had nowhere else to be. She had to get out, she reminded herself. She mused as she entertained the possibility of rejection wargaming the scenario in her head.

She needed a walk in the rain some kind of distraction from the letterbox. She grabbed her coat and went out into the rain, checking three times that the TV was off so she could avoid another confrontation with the ex. She started walking across Vauxhall bridge. There were soldiers and some kind of checkpoint nominally supposed to examine the flow of people to and fro but they seemed largely content to ignore the situation. White blood cells waiting and ready at the sign of an infection but not bothering to look too closely at the crowd. The haggard faces of every London worker white collar replaced with blue collar, or green epilates, it was only really the artefact trade that was booming. The only thing keeping the hum of civilization still singing along.

She walked up through Westminster which was quieter than it had been once. She remembered the civilizational crescendo the first time she came before contact. This was where it all happened. Nowadays the buildings had decayed, now cut off from the blood supply as Parliament migrated north. The song of life migrated to the west or to the space around the embassy. She walked into the west end, past looted Buckingham palace and onto Oxford Street. Even post event life could not kill the commercial heart of London, if anything it was busier now than ever. There were rat skewers being sold at hawker stands, a relic of a time when people were more desperate in the immediate aftermath and something people had developed a taste for. Sausage rolls had migrated back down south and had largely replaced rats for most people except the real connoisseurs. Surplus MREs originally intended as food aid were being sold for profit too. Never ignore the ability of a Brit to find a captive market.

That being said the market stalls were no longer alone and some of the old shop fronts had started to stir with new life. She looked out at a cat café. Trying to avoid the gaze of the obvious alt waitstaff behind the counter. There was something that hurt in seeing that. No, she could not do a job like that. She was better than simple retail. Even an alt like that there was no way they really knew anything about all of it. They probably just fell into some artefact by chance. She had not been delving in the dirt dealing artefacts to the highest bidder. That was better than selling your own body directly anyway. Now a cushy job where she did not know anything about anything. Whiskey would not let herself fall into that. She needed where she could make all those years of time and effort mean something she thought as she pulled her hands into fists.

Walking back to the apartment she felt at points like she was walking to the gallows. She could feel that rejection letter. The image of it filled her with an electric dread that drowned out any other thoughts. She wanted to vomit. Let it all out and fill her brain with distinct and different sensations so she could ignore the pressure of dread. Then she looked at her watch a cheap casio, if she did not get back soon there would not be time for her to make dinner. The dread of dealing with the ex animated her movements. She was home with about an hour to spare and got to work on dinner. The fridge was well stocked which is something that could not be said for most of the haggard faces on the bridge. She started to make a curry, sautéing an onion in butter and adding the curry powder, adding salt, more butter, and some more fresh vegetables and a little bacon as a treat. It turned out well not that she expected any praise. She made herself a small portion and then put the rest in the fridge for the ex later and watched some light TV, a small luxury. There was a whole program on artefacts and their industrial uses. It was comforting. Then she turned on the baseball for a while. Trying to focus on the batter, if he would make contact or not.

When the ex returned there was company there too. A girl hanging around, tall. Less pretty than Whiskey but also an alt, cat ears and a short cat tail with thinner orange hair. The ex gave a glare at Whiskey while walking in and took the girl to the office. Whiskey did not want to listen. They sounded so happy, they were laughing. Behind the locked door. There was a time when Whiskey thought things could still be fixed with the ex, it lasted a while until she got told she was smothering, and told to go out for a walk. Since then, walks had been engrained in her routine as a survival mechanism. Even digging through the dirt looking for artefacts was better than this she thought while letting out a sigh. She was a fire inside her own house, burning it all to ashes. It was always her.

She needed to clean out the rooms.

Chapter 2: Glucose

A letter fluttered through the letter box the next day. She knew what it was, the dark black seal of the ministry emblazoned upon it. Inside was a rejection letter but also an acceptance letter for a similar but unrelated job. One bereft of the stable hours, and government prestige. A job from Kent Holdings a company which provided cheap contract labor to the government, a contract from them. Her eyes darted to the job title of artefact analyst. She had wanted the government job. The office job with nice hours and a regular schedule with Saturday Sunday off, like the woman in her interview. To be given the authority of a long dead crown on her pay slip and not a dollar bill. Given the circumstances though, she could not reject. She would inhabit the ministry’s buildings, work with the ministry and do work for the ministry while it denied and rebuked her. She could not wait to start.

So she spent the morning before training and signing the contract she chiseled herself into the best version she could be. Combing her dark hair and taking extra care to wash out the grease. This would be the day her life would surely change. The office was once again six feet under in the green zone of central London. It had once been ministry land but now as the government receded it was office space for rent. The building itself had seen years of decay and renewal since its being built in the golden days of the empire.

Now it stood as all tombs do, ornate with golden cracks in its face. Cracks dealt by the killing blow. Now all that remained was American money filling the husk with life. These dollars would soon imbibe her bank account. They would become her meals, her life blood, her bones in time. So above as below. The manager was tall with brown hair. He looked tired to his bones as the fresh meat filed in. Mostly normal people. As she looked around she realized she was one of a handful of alts. The man nodded counting up the fresh faces. He had a name lanyard around his neck which read Tim.

“Okay, welcome everyone, I’m with Kent Holdings I’m here to introduce you to your new career!” He spoke with the kind of enthusiasm that could only be mustered by a paycheque motivated mid-level functionary. “So, all of you are our latest artefact analysists, without you there, there would be no-one to catalogue the zone artifacts!” His phrasing was rehearsed but still clunky, a remarkable feat. “In today’s training you will learn how to handle our most common artefacts and how to categorize them.” As the details became more concrete his enthusiasm dropped precipitously as his expertise faded from view. After all he knew all there was to know about trying to sell the company. He explained the pellet system, a paper record which was delivered with details of the artifact she was to analyze. Where it was found and what tests were recommended. He then explained some limited test procedures in vague terms, measuring radiation, and using an atomic clock held at different positions to analyze the time distortion. It was a different approach to what she knew, one of measurements and reasoning and not the blood she knew. All she knew was letting her mind fall into the resonance. Letting the strange warm rocks, and other objects burrow their ways into the synapses of her mind, rhyming with her thoughts as they were consumed for a time. It was clear that the company approach was focused on volume and mechanization, making the analysis as replaceable as possible. The fresh meat was only here because metal arms brought too dear a cost and this situation could only be maintained until silicone minds found a way to make those arms cheaper.

The most common artefacts like her were given the clarity of numbers. Devil’s paste became Type 009. It did file away accuracy to describe them as one number. If you had been in the field as she had you knew how it was more a general family resemblance than that of identical twins between artifacts. Even worse was how they changed over time, such that a twin might one day lose resemblance with its twin. Then again that expendability did make for a very marketable industry. A nodule at the top of her spine purred to remind her of its place in her body, it’s family resemblance. Of what she owed her life to.

Then Tim launched into a rehearsed diatribe about higher level artefacts, hot artefacts. How they were to told to read the pellet and enter all that information into the hitherto fore unmentioned department catalogue computer system and then ensure it was sent to the relevant subdepartment. This was also advised for artefacts that were found to be too hot to handle. At the end of the presentation a bundle of papers and one lone sheet were passed around. One was a schedule, listing every workplace and hours. 11am to 8pm on weekdays and 12pm to 9pm on weekends, with Monday and Tuesday serving as a nominal weekend. She would be working at the Ealing Evaluation center, which had taken over the former town hall. She had wanted to stay around the center of the city where the roads were safer and the symphony of civilization sounded all the clearer. Yet the distance from the ex could be welcome. The bundle of papers was a contract which was almost certainly slightly different to the one sent with her acceptance offer. Yet she had no luxury to dispute it and had not even read the original. Apparently, all of the other alts saw the situation in much the same way as the other 30 sets of eyes in this place. Freshly signed schedules and contracts were reaped from all of them. Whiskey clenched her hands into a fist. A smile ran through her. She had finally made it somewhere at least. She would not be some alt in the gutter, the artefacts embedded into her worth more than the meat that surrounded them.

Moving apartment would be simple enough. The fastest internet speed was now notice boards and payphones. Still, that was more the case for the center. Out west the land value had bled away along with the people. Driven out by beasts, artefacts, and thresholds. The event had been a real disaster for insurers, landlords and estate agents. Many of whom were now uncomfortably resettled in camps up north safe deep inside the green zone. Where land had value and landlords ruled a thousand fiefs. Instead of beasts and artefacts.

Cleaning out the rooms was easier than she had thought. Her whole life spread between shoulder bag, military backpack and cheap wheel along suitcase. The ex’s domain lay within the office and as such Whiskey was at liberty to take whatever she wanted. However, with the exception of one pair of scissors, one tin of S&B curry powder and one jar of peanut butter, and the blanket she lived in, she only took that which she had brought in.

So she embarked on the long walk home to an unknown home. Each step dragged. She could not see her feet beneath her. Stepping the long and ragged road, feeling by feet alone. She traced the landmarks heading west along the riverfront which had been so thoughtfully cleared of development. Step by step and inch by inch her arms straining under the weight of her world. There might have been busses once, taxis and cars but out west save for the government and military maintained supply routes there was no way for those without the ability to turn the soldiers heads the other way. Besides the main routes were more crowded, cumbersome and slower. She had decided she had to make her own way. Over encumbered step after over incumbered step for miles. The river had always been the best guide for in the city. It had guided many a soul to make the city in the first place and still its flow guided any passerby. At one point it had guided great wealth to the city and the river still saw some traffic use to the east but the west was choked by military cordons. It was her alone still being given guidance by the venerable river. Soon she reached Hammersmith and the river curved up like an elbow. This would be the place where she had to show her papers to pass. The arms of the state stretching from the town hall down to the armpit of the river flowing in.

The checkpoint was relatively quiet but of course as with any checkpoint it was not without a line. The workers slowly let the traffic flow through their plaque. The sign stood saying Passage to the Orange Zone this way with an arrow pointing behind the checkpoint. There was another sign too, Documents Ready which had the silhouette of a figure holding a black square with ID written on it. She took out hers early but as she went to do so her heart skipped a beat. Fingers groped unseeingly gasping for the documents she needed in the front pocket of her bag but with no success. It was in the second pocket flanked by her contract. She let out a sigh of relief from the tension. She held the papers tight her hands turning white as she approached the officer. As if the documents were a shield of authority, holding the Kent Holdings logo towards the officer as if some of the corporate authority could protect her like a charm. The man had tired eyes. He did not take long to look at her paperwork.

“Another one of those Kents then?” He said in a deep and deeply tired voice blurring the line between Kent and Cunt. “What address might you be headed to.”

Whiskey felt the weight of those words press into her.

“Are you going to be in company housing same as the rest of them?” He asked.

She should have read her contract and onboarding documentation more clearly. As things stood though his presumption provided an easy escape from this line of questioning.

“Well yes sir.” Whiskey replied eagerly.

“Alright then here’s your Orange Zone permit. Remember the rules on artefacts before you come back through here, sir.” The guard looked down and hesitated before the sir but went through with it anyway deferring to officialdom as all officials do.

She was jolted by the sir, but nodded and walked through. There was an address on her permit. Most likely the company housing. It would be hard to ascertain where the address was in the first place but either way her first port of call would be the town hall. It was the most sensible thing to do but she was not scheduled until tomorrow. She could goof off and find any living pubs in the area. Spend the remainder of her money on whores and whiskey, metaphorically speaking. It was a tempting self-made preposition to buy some nice food and relax in the time before work but she figured it made more sense to have fun after ensuring everything was in its right place.

She continued her journey this time separating from the river and staying nearer the Highstreet. It ran parallel to the great west road and the military corridor therein. There was some foot traffic but little interaction between travelers. This place was an interlude, one track fading out into the next, as the symphony of central London faded into disparate suburban dances. It took her four hours and the sun was starting to hang in the sky. An anxiety she might arrive too late to meet anyone at her new workplace danced in her heart before being snuffed out. The town hall was the older building which still housed the organs of state with a soldier stood outside. Her office was the next building, a modern concrete amalgamation. She crossed the door. It was quiet and it smelled a little like Mildew. Some corridors looked dustier than others and by taking the road more travelled she felt her way towards the Kent Holdings branch. Pasted the cracked concrete the laboratory and office looked much more well maintained than the building at large. Materials that flowed in from long supply chains to make a clean modern interior. There was no-one in attendance at the front desk. Obviously, she was no stranger to the strange sense of inoccupation in a place begging for occupation but the sight of an open can of red bull without a crown of dust was disquieting.

As if summoned by her thoughts there was a noise from down the corridor outside the office and it rumbled closer. It was a man, bearded, stocky with a lanyard around his neck. Detective Inspector Clark Mortazavi. He had a government black seal. She put down her bags near the entrance.

“You don’t know where the rest of your coworkers are do you?” He asked.

“No, actually I’m meant to start tomorrow so I don’t really know any of them.” Whiskey replied her head moving slightly to the right.

“Those fuckers are always like this keeping everyone waiting. He said with a sigh. “We gotta at least look around for them you know I guess.” He said with another sigh and held his hand as he concluded an internal debate.

She gave a nod and followed deeper into the branch. Obviously, there was the ghostly reception but past that there was an atrium and the first room labelled deliveries. There had been some life in this room until recently. A choir with hymn sheets but none of the faithful to sing. With boxes and delivery labels strewn about the place. The next room was labelled examination room two and had all of its requisite tools and writing implements all put away neatly. It had not been used recently they both silently concluded. Exam room one was the last place for them to look. The last possible place. They could be held up in there having a silent sort of break but she doubted it. Her ears twitched and then she felt it. The buzzing at the back of her ear goading her to open the door to cross the threshold.

“God fucking damn it.” Clark held his head in his hands for a second before prying them away to move like a marionette. “Well actually outsourcing to private industry in our case makes sense.” He energy drained out of him as he did an upbeat imitation of someone Whiskey did not know. “God.” He sighed again.

Whiskey stood back for a second digesting the situation. “You got any artefacts on you sir” I think we’re dealing with a can opener behind there.“ She said plainly.

“Yeah, ugh, that’ll be it.” Clark said again assailed by sighs. “Well little artecorp alt, you seem to know better than your coworkers if you ever even get to meet them.” Clark said with a sardonic laugh.

“You got a file?” She asked.

“I guess I don’t carry one around for nothing.” He took out a jagged red rock contained within a home-made walkie talkie contraption. Despite appearances it was reasonably standard issue. He looked at her for a moment as he asked a silent question. Do you want to call it in and wait for help, do you want me to do it?“ Earnest eyes looked with a silent sort of care.

“Let me hook into it. What does it beat to?” Whiskey said clearing the air.

“It’s a 1-2-3, second by second beat.”

He nodded and started to unscrew the knob. The air began to dance counting one two three, etching each number with tremendous energy filling the room. The energy too making the base of her skull tremble. Each number now filtering through into her mind. She could barely think.

“Keep that on.” She shouted above the noise as the air could barely hold any more vibration.

She approached the nondescript wooden office door and took the handle. She looked back and gave her a nod. She stopped for a moment verifying to herself that she was going to do this. She gripped the handle so tight it seared into her before pushing the door forward and crossing the threshold.

Chapter 3: Incisor

The room was dark except the dancing static in the middle, there was a table, but like a pond of bright light, the threshold beckoned. The temperature outside and inside in discordant ignorance of each other. She was starting to sweat from the temperature now. There were three bodies face down propped up by caught limbs, faces below the floor, bathing in a sea of static. Not like she had any of the tools that would have made this easy on her, all long sold to shady arms dealers, digested by the economy and spitting life into her. She approached the threshold like it were a pool. Knees together she took a deep breath out of reflex not need and then took the plunge. It was so tremendously bright. Brighter than bright and so hot it felt cold. Memories were leaking from the delirious faces as their minds, untethered from the body bled white. The binder was starting to fill the space the sound of the numbers fighting against the combined sounds of anything the ear could hear. Impressing reality onto them.

Then there were the faces, every expression a face could show was pressed into them by the relentless static. Around them all a collage of themselves. Faces Whiskey had no way of knowing slowly being drowned in airless delirium. She counted to herself, tracing the beat of the binder 1-2-3. Numbers as a ward against the tide of meaninglessness. Counting seconds in a time out of time to maintain the meaning of a second. A simple thought repeated with enough strength and simplicity to drown it out of her ears. Still the base of her skull trembled with the pulses of an artefact infinitely close and infinitely far. It’s okay, nothing she had not done before. Something she had done for a long time. She could do this. She would not get trapped in here. She did not want to be. This time.

Complex thoughts returning to her brain. Her grip was starting to slip as the colors around her started to contort themselves into memories. Every thought she would ever think stood as the enemy at the gates. What am I doing. Thinking I could just jump in. A creeping dominating insecurity that was going to eat her. One two three, one two three, breathe, breathe, breathe. The first person was a girl with light brown-red hair. Whiskey took her hand and then went for the next one an alt. The last one was a man far away. She only had two arms. In this world of energy she briefly considered imagining herself another arm, inviting the energy into herself letting it fill her body her mind. There would not be enough time to do it perfect. She could not. She focused her eyes and pushed against a self-imagined floor; with legs she clung to against the static. The static danced against her the jaws were starting to close she could feel it. Faster and faster. Trying to navigate her way out of a meaningless maze.

The room on the other side was sterile except from the blood that gushed from a half body. Whiskey for her part had cuts all over her legs. Small and superficial bite marks of reality reasserted. She was holding hands with the two sleeping beauties still breathing. Not a hair on their heads had been hurt. Clark wore a strange face as he looked at Whiskey. Confusion, relief, and anxiety all in equal measure.

“What are you looking at.” She said. The file was still in his hands having just been restrained. “Get a mop, get a bucket, and call it in.”

“You think I didn’t call it in first thing?” He asked perplexed.

“Just making sure.” She said. “Do you need help getting them out of here?” She asked presuming he would move them from the expanding pool of blood and viscera. He was the bigger of their number.

“Right.” He said with a sigh before shouldering them.

Whiskey stood up and felt her legs stinging. There was still a faint buzzing. Where the man’s hips met the ground, a green jagged rock poked out.

“You got a container?” She asked.

“No, but the boys should have one when they get here. I called them a couple of hours ago now.” He said sighing again.

“I was under two hours in there? Not too bad.” She said. After all some people had spent hears within a threshold. She shook involuntarily at the thought, the artefact at the base of her brain pulsing.

“It was loud having a file like that blaring all that time. You’d have thought I’d gone deaf.” He said.

“You could have left. Anyway the container there’s a second artefact near the half man. I don’t know what it is.” She paused and her ears twitched. She heard it pulsing nice and easy. “It sounds stable.”

Then as if to prove her wrong in front of him the air started to fill. Her ears darted involuntarily towards the sound but it was not that green rock, it was the crate on the desk. Drop and run was her first thought but before her brain could process self-preservation her hands were already around the crate. Her muscles were tensing, her calves bent. There was only legs and muscle in motion. Meat in motion, motionless half man. Her eyes were already looking for a room to drop and run from the little box of horrors. Down the corridor the space around her started to fill with sound. A beckoning temporal delirium. There was an old waiting room full of dusty stale air. She dropped and ran and as she crossed the door the room flashed with brilliant light, she sweated from the sudden increase in temperature. She had just made it.

She swaggered back to the branch. Clark looked dumbfounded. “Well, that was just fine wasn’t it.” He looked at her trying to make some reprimand.

“The new artefact it could’ve interacted badly with it.” She looked for justification 2. “Also he could have been, you know the body.”

He looked at her with a face of resigned anger.

“I know you think I’m stupid.” She said.

“Whatever.” He pressed the mop into her and sighed. “Come on. Let’s get on with it.”

She nodded.

The blood had pooled looking much like the static threshold. The wound resembling its own cause. She had never gotten a clear look at him and any photo would be seen through the lens of the twisted bloody torso. She had always heard about how much blood the body had. How many miles the guts could run for if laid out. Those numbers felt meaningless as the guts stretched out. Shit and pennies. It could not have been longer than fifteen minutes to clean up half his life blood. It could have only been like this was what she told herself. Letting it in, losing her shape in that void. There would be four half bodies bleeding out had she done so.

“I guess it’ll be for the boys to do the rest then.” She said.

“Already on their way, I’m pretty sure they’ll have a corporate representative with them.” He said simply. “What a way to start working huh.” He slapped her back.

“Yeah I guess.”

“I wonder what it does.” He gestured to the object.

“It’s really not that active.” She looked over at it again. Now it was yellow and still jagged but forming itself into a cup.

There was a stir from the lobby. The seating area repurposed for the two co-workers. Clark and Whiskey got up and prepared for the inevitable. The girl with red brown hair was the first with a deep visceral scream which petered out quickly. Clark knelt down in front of her.

“You’re okay. It’s okay.” Clark said.

The air was filled with her deep breaths and the breaths of the sleeping altie.

“Miss what’s your name.” As he looked for a lanyard. Then he turned for a second and started again. “You’re here at your work it’s been between two and three hours, you live in London in Ealing and you have wonderful long hair.” He spoke so softly Whiskey thought.

The girl replied with a bug eyed glare but gradually as meaning returned to her mind she started to come back.

“I’m Jessica, so I guess I’m still on the clock then if it’s only been that long.”

“Probably but you shouldn’t get up too quickly. Easy, easy.” Clark said.

“I guess if they’re going to dock my pay anyway for the branch closure.” She said with a sigh. She got up to sit properly in a chair.

“Where’s Mark.” She said looking around.

The name, the half torso, the sudden pull on Whiskey’s stomach. Clark sat for a moment trying to formulate a response. Jessica tried to read his face and then Whiskey’s. Silence filled the room.

She answered her own question. “He’s just in the back right? I mean it was his idea, dealing with that one, he knew what to do right.” She said calmly but authoritatively.

Clark tried to go around finding the right words. Whiskey stayed quiet. There was nothing there she wanted to say for now. The silence was kinder than anything else.

“How long had you known Mark.” Clark asked.

“Maybe about 8 months, he’d been at the company longer.” She spoke simply.

Clark spent a minute trying to calibrate this new information into the line he was concocting but Whiskey couldn’t wait anymore.

“I’m sorry but it’s really your own fault if you go asking questions you don’t want the answers to.” Whiskey said. Her gut sank for a moment.

“I see.” Jessica replied simply. “Makes sense.”

A less weighty silence bled of its overhanging anticipation followed before the start of the second scream. This one was longer and more strained. Her voice started to crackle burned away by its own length. She screamed her lungs out trying to expel meaningless memories like a drowned swimmer. Then her head darted around the room scanning the new faces and Jessica.

Before Clark could say anything she had opened her mouth to start babbling. “Where’s am I is Mark?” She asked. The first words stumbling foal like from her lips. Clark looked down at her name lanyard.

“It’s okay, you’re okay, hello Skye, you live in London, you live in Ealing, you work for Kent holdings and you have dirty blonde hair, mouse ears and a thin tail.” Again his soft voice trying to cushion her fall back into reality. “The time is 4:56pm.” He said looking at his watch, which was in military time.

She held her head for a minute. “Who are you?”

“I’m Clark, Clark Mortazavi.” He spoke with a slight embarrassment. “I’m a detective inspector. I’m here to help.” He did so with the un warranted confidence of his own authoritative title in his eyes.

Whiskey felt a twang of resentment ripple in her stomach. He had barely helped at all. She swallowed it down. He did put out the file even if it was mostly all her.

“Good morning Skye.” Whiskey said with a smile and interrupting Clark and Skye.

The girl looked over scanning Whiskey with her bright blue eyes. “I’m sorry but who are you?”

“The name’s Whiskey don’t wear it out.” She said with a smile smug and forced and unearned. The smile of a hero waiting for their adoring crowd.

“You’re altered too.” Skye said simply. “Where’s the guy, sorry I’m just wondering.”

“He’s gone Skye.” Said Jessica.

“Oh right yeah uh.” Even after waking up the words, she chose stumbled awkwardly as they filled the room. “I heard actually did you know that artefact analysists are usually safer than scavengers and redeemers. If you read the statistics actually it’s clear that. Sorry.” She spoke with an awkward laugh leaving her thought unfinished.

“In about 20 minutes from now a cleanup and debrief team will be arriving so we are to sit tight till then.” Clark said.

Sky and Jessica nodded and then he looked over at Whiskey. “That goes for you too you mangy cat.”

“Who are you calling mangy? I’m not the one with the scraggly beard and beer belly.” Whiskey said with a laugh.

“You’ll have a beer belly soon enough working this job.” Clark said.

“Whatever dude.” Whiskey shot back.

Jessica interrupted the playground squabbles with a question. “Can I smoke here now?” She asked, a pink Bic lighter and menthol cigarettes in hand.

“It’s probably not legal still and look I don’t care but your manager will care if he smells smoke but you know it’s up to you.” Clark said. “Though I could really go for a smoke. Hey cat, you smoke too?” He asked

“Today’s the day for it.” She said with a smile.

“Skye?” Clark turned and asked.

“Well actually I’m sorry but I’ve never smoked and I think it’s a bit smelly and gross even when Jess smokes her ones, they smell weird.” Skye meandered.

“Well, 3-1 let’s get out of here a sec and take a quick smoke break.” He gestured out and led the way, then Jess, Skye and Whiskey holding the rear.

Whiskey started to awkwardly fumble for her lighter before finding it. The nice firm construction of a zippo lighter in her hands. They lined up against the concrete with Skye standing a bit away from them. Even the end of Britain could not kill the Anglo-American tobacco company. Regular Marlboro reds for the detective, Marlboro lights for the cat, and self-rolled menthols for the girl. Whiskey panicked a moment hoping that she had remembered to fuel her lighter, after a few agonizing clicks it lit. Clark was already underway and so was Jessica. There was her first long drag. She could hear a slight crackle from the tiny flame. Then like all the muck in her soul was being expelled she exhaled. As if the toxic smoke could replace it all. They stood in silence. Clark was done first, then Whiskey and then Jessica. The sun started to set and the cold was coming in.

Clark looked at his watch and waited, they would be here soon, no point in going back in. He just let the smoke hang in the air a few minutes. Then the convoy arrived, one military truck and one grey sedan. A column of tired eyes. They advanced into the building with Clark but the three employees were left standing listless outside to be debriefed by a manager with the same tacky colored Kent Lanyard. It was Tim again from training.

He stood up with a strange smile stretched by contorted muscles in his face and absent from his eyes.

“Okay everyone hello!” He was disgustingly upbeat. “Welcome Whiskey, sounds like you had an eventful first day of work.” He said laughing at himself. “So, I heard that there had been a little bit of an issue here, there have been a couple of branches, actually. So sometimes the pellets we get they can have a couple of issues here and there you know. So we need to be really careful and remember that safety is number one. Call things in if there is any indication of anything going wrong okay?” His intonation rose to a dizzying high at the end. His words strangling any potential conflict in the crib. “Oh and smoking, so there have been some complaints from customers about some of our workers smelling like smoke, so if you could refrain from it while on the clock.” As though these problems were of equal importance. Everyone gave him a nod in response as they were lined up in front of the wall.

“Okay so also welcome Whiskey, so you’re signed up for the company housing right?” He said stepping towards the group of them a little. “So everyone else will be able to direct you.”

As he stepped forward he counted them. “Where’s Mark?” He asked. “So is he?”

Silence filled the void of an answer, Tim breathed in for a moment, before smiling again. He took out a notepad and made a note.

“Then, I guess we can get you moved in, Whiskey. I’m going to take inventory in a minute. Come on, lets help two can help him to his new room. Here’s your new key! He said holding them out towards her.” Tim nodded at Skye and Jessica

Hearing the stray pronoun shot through her nerves. A flash of sudden pain that made her recoil. They went back into the building towards the branch, the door was closed but just outside was a neat pile of bags. Her life in three bags. The men walked out with half a life in one bag. She shouldered her bags and Skye decided to linger but Jessica directed her towards the company housing. It was a small set of terraced houses that had undergone some degree of restoration, all in a row. They had likely been spared much of the wounds of anywhere else, and any superficial perforations had been healed. She held out the keys, around here there were few homes with keys. She pressed it into her thumb.

“This one should be yours. Well it might be.” Said Jessica as they walked up towards an orange house that was one offset from the end. Whiskey fiddled with the keys but they did not seem to fit right. “Tim probably screwed up with the keys, I guess I’m not surprised.” She said with a smug laugh.

“Are you sure this is it.” Whiskey asked.

“Yeah, he just gave you the wrong keys, it’s probably unlocked anyway.” She pushed down on the handle and laughed at Whiskey. “See, it was open all along.”

Inside there were signs of recent habitation, a coat hung on a hook. Chairs strewn around, a mattress, a book open on a page.

“Are you sure this is supposed to be so furnished?” Whiskey asked.

“That’s weird, maybe.” Jessica stepped forward a bit but still behind Whiskey. “Oh.” She made the noise. “This must be.” The silence completed the sentence.

There was the shape of a man, an outline in chalk around his possessions. His skin still filled the air. Cloaked in the air, the rotting DNA of a dead man. The remains of days old skin cells being the only part of him that would once again cross the threshold of this place. Nothing besides remained. They left that place cloaked in the same self-completing silence.

“So, you must be this one.” She gestured towards the end of terrace. A white townhouse.

Whiskey walked up to the door. The key fit this time. Crossing the threshold into an empty pair of lungs. There was no furniture. Wooden floor and white walls. A toilet but nothing else. None of the life-making conveniences.

“I guess he did give you the right key huh.” Jessica said.

Whiskey nodded. It smelled dry inside, clean inside.

Jessica gave an awkward look around. “I guess I’ll leave you to it. I’ll see you at work tomorrow, remember your shift, 11am.” As she made her way to leave Whiskey turned towards her.

“Wait.” She said with a hint of desperation.

Jessica turned back. “Hmm?”

“Where am I supposed to sleep? There aren’t any curtains either. It’s too bright.” Whiskey asked without knowing what to do. An innocent look on her face. “I could make do but.” She was more addicted to the creature comforts of an American salary than she could ever admit. Her rough and tumble personality tumbling apart with her actions. The single step and outstretched desperate arm.

“Well.” Jessica said. Letting her word hang in the air. “We could go looking around there are always old things in old houses, left behind, it might be a bit of a trek though, this area has mostly been picked clean last time Skye and I checked but.”

“I don’t mind going a bit far I suppose.” Whiskey said not realizing the implication.

“This is such a horrible thing to say.” Jessica said.

That utterance made Whiskey realize. Crystalizing the thought. Really there was nothing else for it. Every day more and more of the orange zone had whatever was useful picked out of it. As the waves of safety carried away anything of value. Condensing the city into its core. There really was no telling how far away a mattress would be, and the cost of transportation if she had to go as far to buy from the markets the furniture that had been picked clean. Money, she did not have right now. Really there was nothing else for it. There were no other methods. Not if she headed towards the center anyway, but heading further away that was its own risks and how far would she go to risk herself to avoid being haunted and cursed for graverobbing. The possibility of superstition more seductive than the probability of running into some nasty orphaned artefact, or worse.

“We could go and retrieve his mattress from his house. It’s not like he’ll be using it anymore.” Whiskey giving shape to the allusion.

“Yeah. You’re right.” Handing over the responsibility for who came up with the idea.

The shared agreement of complicity as they headed to rob the house. Still they could not bring themselves to speak in his house. As though they were robbing from a man sleeping upstairs. In a way they were. The bed he had was a simple mattress on a floor and a few blankets strewn around. It was old and had been taken from some other house. Probably a while ago when there was more to take and more danger in the taking around here. They also found blackout curtains and as they carted them over they made one last pass. Whiskey looked at the book on the table. Do androids dream of electric sheep, by Phillip K Dick. She took the book for her own. Whiskey thought to herself. A table and a small wooden sofa were also pilfered. Then some cooking tools. All the necessary tools for life somewhere. At the end of their shopping spree they were both tired as Jessica took some things back to her own apartment.

“Thanks for the help.” Whiskey said.

“Not really, besides it’s not like you didn’t help me take- get a thing or two myself.” Jessica said.

“It was my pleasure.” Whiskey replied.

“Your pleasure to rob a dead man wow. Even his book, can’t his ghost be given any entertainment.” She said starting to laugh.

“Dead men read no tales Jessica.” Whiskey said wagging her finger.

“What about undead men? What if he’s going to be haunting his own house.” Jessica replied back.
