“Well if he’s haunting anyone he’s probably haunting me, after all I stole his previous resting place.” Whiskey gestured as though to a crowd.

Jessica laughed. “We’re so terrible.”

“If we weren’t terrible we wouldn’t go grave robbing.” Whiskey said.

“No. We’re terrible because we went grave robbing then we started joking about it.” Jessica said the laughter stopping.

“Well.” Whiskey stood there for a moment. “We are terrible people, terribly, going to. Going to terribly enjoy our new creature comforts without being haunted.”

Jessica gave a laugh. “That doesn’t really work.”

“I know.” Whiskey replied with a smile.

“I’ll see you tomorrow, Whiskey.” Jessica said with a wave. Whiskey waved back.

Whiskey crossed the threshold into her new abode. Her first abode. Permanent and solid and all hers. Not hers. It was the company’s house. None of the furniture here was hers. All that was hers was her life in three bags. The mattress was not hers. He still clung to it. It was all over it. The dust, his skin. Something that could only be found in two places in this world. All that remains of a life, bagged and scattered. She starred up at the unknown ceiling. She dissolved into sleep, as the last microorganisms processed the last chunks of that man.

Chapter 4: Intravenous

The first day on the job. She chiseled her legs into walking the ten minutes or so to the town hall. She wormed her way into the corridors and past the room with the threshold thrashing away. Held back by a brown wooden door and a sign behind it. Orphan artefact it read. She wondered about passing the door. Descending again into its depths. Her hand contorted around the door handle. Those two years of empty sweet nothing called to her like an old lover from the end of the receiver. The dangling enticing light of an angler fish. Coaxing her towards.

“Morning.” Said Jessica with a wave.

Whiskey turned towards her. “Good morning.” With a particular three finger wave she held up towards her head.

“They really cleaned everything up quickly didn’t they.” She gestured at the building.

Whiskey had not noticed but it was true, the dust itself seemed to have been disturbed and driven off by the commotion. A shabbiness held back by life bubbling away in the building’s halls.

“I suppose.” Whiskey responded.

“They came this morning too. They cleaned it out. I guess it’s good that we got what we could, before it was all sold off or dumped.” Jessica said.

“Probably dumped.” Whiskey said simply. Nothing besides remained of that man.

“This company? They’ll try to squeeze out every last pound. Anything they can take. Everything they can take from you. That’s just how they are.” Jessica said.

“It’s usually cheaper to dump nowadays, finding a buyer and the cost of time and selling it in its own way.” Whiskey said. “So, we’re not even worth it, there are no extra parts to be reprocessed. There is only the scrapheap.”

“I guess so.” Jessica said.

Silence crept in for a moment. As they both wondered into thinking of the man on the scrapheap. The half man. Materially excised from being. Whiskey trembled for a moment. Should she say sorry. The guilt was starting to eat her thoughts. Chewing and spitting her out. Her hands trembled in the mouth of her feelings.

“I don’t want to work today.” Jessica said. “I’m killing myself.”

Whiskey looked her up and down as she said it simply but with a smile.

“Is it really that bad anyway?” Whiskey asked simply.

They started walking towards the office and talking.

“I guess it’s not that bad. I’ve done worse, I’ve done better.” Jessica said.

“That Tim guy is interesting.” Whiskey said.

“He’s not very good at his job.” Jessica replied. “Though he rarely shows up here.”

“I see. That’s a relief then.” Whiskey said.

They crossed into the branch. It was even cleaner than yesterday, whatever contractors they had made short work of the place. Skye was already sat down behind the front desk. She had a computer in front of her that she was going through.

“Morning.” Skye said looking up for a minute.

Whiskey gave a thumb and two finger wave before continuing towards her desk while Jessica replied and started talking to Skye. The front was arranged with two desks, one in front and one behind. On the second desk there were two chairs for Jessica and Whiskey with a clipboard sitting on the table. Whiskey took a look at it. It was expected deliveries throughout the day and the kinds of artefacts to be examined. Devil paste, devil paste, stopper, file, listed on her schedule. She let out a sigh as the minutes started to count down towards the start of the work day. She took out her book and started to read for the scant few minutes she had.

“Um Whiskey it’s um, time so, I think it’s already in examination room one.” Skye said.

Whiskey nodded back.

“Ugh.” Jessica said as she made her way to exam room two.

As Whiskey walked in she saw the huge wooden crate standing alone on the table. She looked at the pellet for it. Devil paste straight from Bristol, fairly by the numbers stuff. Her artefact at the base of her skull seemed to pulse a little with it. She took out the examination gloves. They were new but also cheap. Still any barrier to keep that stuff from her skin would be nice. She put her hand in it feeling for any solid particles and noting the consistency of it. It was more on the liquid side compared to some varieties and had a slight pinkish hue. The smell was unmistakable though. Ozone through the air. Next was to test the friction of it. The best varieties of devil paste she had seen could minimize friction to practically nothing. There was a tribometer in the corner and as she let the paste fall harmlessly through her fingers she gathered a sample in a syringe. It was a tricky material to work with not least because it did not adhere to anything. Except human any human cells that is. As she went over to the tribometer she noticed that the tip that read the friction did not have anything to adhere the sample. She took a cotton swab to the inside of her cheek and applied it to the tip. Then the syringe. Then she set the machine to run.

It was a relatively standard batch reducing friction from the control by about two thirds. Not perfect but far from nothing. This scientific miracle which crawled from a toxic hole in the middle of western England. She took out a timer and waited ten minutes before testing again. This time to measure the stability in the batch. It was the same. A good batch or at least relatively stable.

The next batch proceeded the same way, swab, tribometer, record. There was an awful lot of devil’s paste. Soon enough it was lunch break unpaid of course. She left the examination room. Both of the other girls seemed to have already taken off and again the room was filled with the empty sense of people who until recently were there.

The back of her neck it pulsed. She could feel the threshold just across the room calling out to her. Lacking anything to eat for lunch perhaps she could satiate her desire for sensation across the way. Why not. It was something she had done before. She could time it. Pull herself back out. She looked around the cupboards for something to use as a binder would have been nice. It did not take her long. There was a military radio, standard issue but still reasonably new. It had to be from the cleanup crew yesterday. Strewn around as the last remains of the squad. She turned it on and it was already tuned to the frequency they used. What an easy way to listen into their communications. Shame she was just using it as a tool to get her fix. A silver spoon taken from a celebrity chef.

The hum of comings and goings and status reports would pave her way back. She took it to the room. It was hot. It was loud. It was all over everything. There was nothing that was not the threshold. She paused at the edge. Her hands gripping the edge of reality. Then she lent forward. It was an instant it was everything it was all the time in the world that swept her up in a wave. She was subsumed. The faint sound of military checkpoints the only shell to keep herself from spilling into the energy. Yet still through the semi permeable membrane meaning she clad herself in she could feel it. The tide. The delightful feeling of everything at once. Every nerve filling with sensation. She could get lost again in it. The jaws opened and closed above. If she mistimed it she could get decapitated. If she mistimed it she could get fired. At least getting decapitated would bleed all meaning from being fired just as it bled all meaning from her corpse. A meaningless pile of meat. A meaningless mass of energy wrapping her away singing sweet nothings into a permanent end.

She did not know the time. It felt like only seconds. Minutes maybe. Hours and years maybe. There was only now. Part of her brain tugged at her. If the radio slipped she would lose it all. She struggled her way out. Coming back to the surface. Sweat clung to her as the exhilaration faded and the pain returned. What was she doing again. This pointless dance of meaningless nothing. It was a moment or respite from reality. Now she was swept back up in a different wave. She clung to the door handle. She was iron. Like the handle. She would endure. She told herself that. Until the tears stopped.

“You’re a little uh late.” Said Skye with an awkward laugh as Whiskey walked back in.

She nodded. Looked at her watch. She was three minutes late. She was good. She was still whole.

Jessica looked up at her. She gave a look that seemed confused as she tried to decode the expression on Whiskey’s face, before giving up.

The next artefact rolled in. It was a battery. These things rolled in fairly often and were simpler to study than devil’s paste. Something mercifully easy at least. She just hooked it up to a voltmeter. Then she tested it with an ammeter. Then she waited to see if the current or voltage changed and if so by how much. Then she tested different wires and interfaces. It was electrical work sure, but it did not feel as dangerous to her. It was a known quantity. Then the day rolled onto its end. The end of her first day. Skye was the one who was responsible for closing shop by the looks.

Jessica stood by the exit with a bag and coat wrapped around her. Along with a lighter and some cigarettes. She looked over at Whiskey as she made her way to leave.

“Hey, want a smoke?” Jessica asked.

“Why? And besides I don’t have any on me.” Whiskey replied.

“I can give you one. Besides it’s your first day, you know.” Jessica was tired but trying to create some kind of enthusiasm.

“I could do one.” One being the number of cigarettes Whiskey could take without feeling guilty. “But I’ll pay you back, I promise.”

“Okay.” Jessica said plainly.

They walked outside to where they had yesterday. That concrete façade where the cigarette butts marked their place.

“How was lunch today? Did you get to explore the area at all?” Jessica asked.

“Oh uh I didn’t do a whole lot, I mostly stayed in the old town hall.” Whiskey replied trying to play off awkwardness as being casual.

“There’s some stalls a bit away you know. They do some nice deals. We should go.” Jessica said.

Whiskey stood there trying to figure out if she had been exposed for her illicit activities or not. Jessica stared at her again. Whiskey instinctively started scratching the back of her neck as silence started to creep in.

“Oh yeah, how is your room.” Jessica asked.

“It’s fine. It’s plain, it’s pretty clean. One of the cleanest rooms I’ve ever lived in. The mattress is comfy too. Thanks for helping me out with it yesterday.” Whiskey said with the biggest smile she could tease from herself.

“It wasn’t doing any good just sitting there.” Jessica said. “Before I got here, I was doing odd jobs in the green zone you know. I worked as a waitress, I worked at a care home, I worked the army kitchens.”

“Why’d you come to work with artefacts?” Whiskey asked without even thinking the question bypassing her brain.

“I’d always been interested in them. My dad he was in the army, and he was always talking about what they found you know. Curiosity and the cat and all.” Jessica said gesturing around.

Even with all of the options in the world someone else ended up here just the same. At this dingy suburb of a city bled dry bleeding your days away to a job that could well kill you.

“They are something else. How long have you been here?” Whiskey asked.

“Like a year now. Same old same old categorization work though nowadays I work on the pickles and send them off. It’s way easier than categorization itself and a bit safer too. Once you’ve been here a few months you should see if they could train you up for it. I mean the training is a joke.” Jessica said. “You know my mum went crazy when I told her I took this job. Telling me I’ll be eaten by some beast down here. She was okay with it in the end though. I wasn’t the first one of us to work on them after all and I was always dad’s favorite, so he was able to get her to get it.”

Whiskey nodded. She took the last puff of her cigarette. A whole conversation where she could avoid talking about herself. Good job. Good job for saving this girl too. It was her after all. A girl full of thoughts and people who cared about her somewhere. Not an artefact embedded in her. Her value was not given by the state, by an artefact classification board, by a cv. Whiskey felt like she needed another cigarette as the that thought made contact with her brain. A strobing wire burning into already fried circuits. She was normal.

“I should get going.” The words stumbled out of Whiskey. She wanted to talk about it. About what hung over her. She wanted to but she could not. She was better than that. Better than burdening other people with the weight of her problems. What good would it do. To shock them, to burn them.

“We live right next to each other. We can talk and walk.” Jessica said with a smile.

Whiskey nodded.

“The company can be unforgiving. One time I was sick, so I went into town to get a doctors note which took forever since there aren’t any good clinics nearby and they still took out a big chunk of my pay. Today I know you were only a little late but be careful about that if it gets reported then they can take from your pay.” Jessica said.

“How much?” Whiskey asked.

“Like 5% for any lates. I mean Skye probably won’t report it at least immediately.” Jessica said.

“Why would she ever even report it?” Whiskey asked.

“She can be a bit of a jobsworthy.” Jessica said with a shrug. “She’s lovely but she can get judgy.”

“That’s good to know.” Whiskey said.

“They can send you to other branches too. I used to get sent into the Reading one a lot the one near the train line. They pay for any travel expenses or hotels which is nice though but it can be super annoying to spend so much time travelling. It is nice to see more of the zone though.” Jessica said.

“How often were they sending you?” Whiskey asked.

“When I started I think I spent two or three months filling in out there. There were a few accidents and I had to fill in until they got a replacement. Still it can be nice there. Reading is more like in town, there’s a nice bit near the train depot where people come through. Some pubs even a nice beer garden that was great summer last year.” Jessica said.

“I’ve been through there a couple of times too. Going through out west.” Whiskey said.

“How far have you been into the zone?” Jessica asked.

“I went to Bristol, furthest I’ve gone is Plymouth. That place is still a bit up in the air.” Whiskey said.

“I’ve always wanted to go to see Plymouth. It’s the last frontier.” Jessica said.

“It’s an interesting place. The military isn’t so involved over there just because of the logistics involved so it’s a little town mostly of alts in the ruins of the marina. It’s a great place to barter and there’s always ships coming in to dock. You can see the black zone too just across the Tamar. Lighting up the sky in a dozen different colors. I want to go back.” Whiskey said. “Last time was maybe three or four years ago.”

“Alts huh. You know I kinda wondered if the new employee was going to be an alt.” Jessica said out loud.

“How come?” Whiskey asked.

“I heard there’d been layoffs at the Ministry and alts seem to get filtered into the company. There’s Skye already but a couple of people in Reading too. I guess it seemed like I was the only person who was not an alt who got hired right now.” Jessica said.

“Well at training there were a few norm- normal people.” Tripping over her words not to say normies. “It was fairly even though I guess it means we’re disproportionately common in this company.”

They arrived back at that street without Whiskey even realizing the journey.

“Hey so there’s a place to get cheap booze just near here if you want to go and get some.” Jessica said.

Whiskey could not turn down a drink. “That sounds good. Though I don’t have a whole lot of money.” She said counting out the bills she had in the back of her mind.

“You can pay me back later.” Jessica said.

“I have enough for a round or two I suppose anyway.” Whiskey said.

There were no street lights anymore save for the light of a sign which she assumed was the destination. The sign read KEC from a KFC sign repurposed and illuminated with flood lights that had been left behind or pilfered from a checkpoint. The meaning of the letters came into view as they reached the threshold. Kingly Ealing Cans. It lived up to the reputation.

“Jess back in the house.” The man behind the counter said. “We’ve got some new good stuff in today. We got army cocktails, some other canned cocktails, the sparks mojito is pretty good, we’ve got some army beer, other beer.” He waved in the direction of some pallets and some refrigerators that were miraculously on. It was all cans as if the store owner had declared war against the concept of a glass bottle.

“Do you have anything in the cooler?” Jessica asked.

“We have some army beer and some cocktails, there’s the lemony one, as always.” He said with a knowing look.

“I’ll go for that then.” Jessica said.

He nodded “And for you new blood.” Facing towards Whiskey.

“You have any ale, any amber ale or anything?” Whiskey asked.

“Let’s see.” He looked up into the air as he itemized the stock in the back of his mind. “We might.” He rooted around in the shelves behind him reading the cans. It was a navy and blue can. Room temperature set on the counter. He then stooped down and took out two tall cans of yellow lemon canned premade cocktail with cooled condensation as they were set down at the counter. “That’ll be a fiver.” He said naming the price. Of course, there was a register but the calculator on it sat dormant.

She placed a fiver on the counter and the goods were exchanged. Soon they were heading back in the direction of the two newly renovated houses.

“That’s a good shop. Thanks for telling me.” Whiskey said with a smile.

“It is nice isn’t it.” Jessica paused. “Still amber ale? I couldn’t drink anything like that.”

“Well, it’s simple I like beer with flavor. Something nice and comforting.” Whiskey said holding the can carefully.

“I tried that army beer once. It’s okay.” Jessica said.

“It’s fizzy water. It’s awful, it’s got nothing no flavor.” Whiskey said with a smug smile.

“I’ve been drinking mixed drinks ever since I started drinking.” Jessica said. “The first time at a house party with some of the other kids at my school. Vodka mixed with orange juice, or with cola. The only thing a group of fourteen-year-olds could think of.”

“Well I guess you went to more of a party school then.” Whiskey said.

“There were only about forty people in my year. I was in a military school, my dad was in the army so.” Jessica said.

Whiskey looked out for a minute. “I wasn’t cool enough to get invited to parties till I was a little older. I never really. Well. Anyway.” Whiskey cut herself off. “Did you live on base or anything?”

“We did. It was a school attached to the base so.” Jessica replied.

The conversation sat there in a lull for a minute.

“I made most of my friends back then. I still talk to them. They think about coming to visit me but there’s all the paperwork with coming down here and it is safe but they think it’s not that safe. I should go back and see them at some point, but we’re always texting.”

Whiskey flinched a little. Bubbling at the back of her throat there was something burning something trying to burn its way past her lips. A searing building pain. The acrid taste of molten metal as she tried to recast her heart. “That’s nice.”

“I remember the first time I tried gin with them. It was so gross we threw up from the taste and not the booze. We did so much stupid stuff in school. There was the pass out challenge, you’d try and do a bunch of stuff and stand up super-fast so that you’d pass out. Anyway, this one girl I knew had a seizure from it.” Jessica said smiling about it.

“I see.” Whiskey said. “I guess that sounds fun.”

They turned a corner at they were back at their little enclave. Whiskey stayed back a bit while Jessica fiddled with her key. She stood awkwardly on the threshold not sure if Jessica really meant for her to follow. Jessica looked back and nodded towards the door inviting her in.

Whiskey was first hit with the smell. The smell of someone else’s home, something that went amiss in a clean room or in the place you actually lived. It was not particularly pleasant and the room was not quite to the level of a hoarder but it was more encumbered than one would expect from a room. Filled with scavenged objects of a lost world. There was a sofa, a nice kitchen, though with washing up still in the sink haphazardly. Whiskey took a seat on the sofa which looked clean enough. She opened her beer, room temperature and started to whisk the night away with drinking and listening to Jessica talk about relationships. She was there as an ear to listen deliberately so. Killing every urge that bubbled up to compel her to speak or intervene. It would do more harm than good she told herself. All night long she told herself. Imbibing the alcohol, imbibing the words of a once stranger and now tentative friend to distract from the rumbles of her mind. The short-circuiting wires, the blown fuses, now put at rest by the words and the chemicals diffusing through her ears through her blood. It was all she could want. A follow up drink took the form of a bottle of fireball. An old friend of hers. It was half drunk, and decidedly room temperature. As she took it into her mind it ran against the strobing fuses.

She wanted to scream, to call out to the world and let the energy dissipate somewhere. The current surging through her nerves compelling the formation of tears. She tried to keep it contained to resist it to try and focus on the saccharine sweetness of the booze such that the sensation could resist the current. Jessica looked at her with a gaze that Whiskey did not fully understand for a moment. A look in her eyes. A genuine concern. Finally, the resistors came into effect. She was pathetic crying like this over nothing for no reason in front of someone else. What was she doing being here. She was iron she was steel. Tears still melted through.

“Are you alright?” Came the question down as a hammer blow. She had been seen. How much would she pay for this concern. Her mind scrambled to see the drop in estimation she would experience for acting so pathetically.

“I’m fine.” She spoke holding back the shaking burning crying compulsion trying to wring the tears from her.

“I see.” Jessica gave a reserved utterance. Acknowledging the distance not knowing how to bridge it. “Do you want a smoke?”

“Please.” Whiskey looked up. “I’ll make it up to you. I’ll give you one of mine. I’ll buy you a pack.” Said like begging for someone to stop hitting her. Words that had been seared into her.

“It’s okay.” Jessica said not wholly sympathetically but not wholly neutrally.

They stepped outside the door. Jessica lit up her own cigarette and then looked over to Whiskey. She took her first and deepest breath. The nicotine and numerous other chemicals making their way from the cigarette through the filter through her mouth and into her lungs before spreading into the blood and brain. Interfering with all the signals of pain. A moment of respite. She would smoke two cigarettes in silence before making her goodbyes and heading home.

Chapter 5: Homeostasis

The calendar rolled over onto the next day and she got dressed. Cleared the sweat off of her face, washed the dried vomit out of her hair. Stifled the body’s cries to vomit. She quickly cleaned off the dried vomit from the side of the bed. Clearly she had thrown up in her sleep. Something which may have had lethal consequences had she been unlucky. For now at least she had luck on her side. She tried not to think about it. The sting in her nostrils warning her. The faint taste of cinnamon at the back of her throat. It felt like her body was trying to shake her consciousness from it. Ridding it of further damage inflicted by the mind held atop that gnarled spine. The artefact in the base of her neck seemed to fizz as if to reject the fanciful notion of an aching stomach. Time to get up and get out.

She looked presentable enough as she stumbled out the door. She doubled back to lock the door again. She stumbled a half-remembered route. Her feet remembering more than her half functioning mind. She walked towards the branch to find that she was embarrassingly early. There was not a single soul in occupation. She stood outside waiting awkwardly. Trying to cover up the urge to vomit on her workplace.

After about twenty minutes Skye appeared around the corner.

entry.title: New Story
entry.content:
Chapter 1

The day was bright and cold. The building was insulated enough even though some windows had been perforated. The monster that had cut those lacerations had long receded, contained beyond fences and soldiers. Out to the west. In the zone. It was a Sunday. The time was nearing 9am and the sun continued to climb impeded by cloud or the worries of mortals that lay underneath its outstretched bounds. Inside the room there lay a mattress. The sink worked somehow, having been patched many times. There were other mattresses below, and they all stood unoccupied save the one on this, top floor. Next to the sink there was a bottle of whiskey it had been drained three quarters and a sticky shot glass next to it. On the floor below there were bottles in various states of drainage, the glasses were still sticky but the hands that had held them had long since moved on. In the ruined townhouse there remained only one soul.

On her arm a scar ran down, it was thin and jagged. She could still recall the pain when she had to remove part of her own skin. She still carried the knife with her. She told herself it was intended to be used in the same manner if she had to do the same procedure again. She looked in the mirror and saw her face through the lens of a headache. Her bones creaked her muscles pulled taught as strings and ached for the exertion. She took out her black notepad to look over the details again. She was after all meeting with a client later today at Scotland Yard. She had her ID laid out nicely to get through the cordon and into Whitehall. Most people would have a name on their ID, a date of birth, and then other functional information, expiration date and issuance date and type. She had a designation, W8194-HB. She did not have a date of birth, instead, d.o.c. which stood for date of contact, 08/03/2019. The gender was listed as M bracketed. The specter of legal malehood still asserted even as any claim to humanity was revoked. As if the final passing shot removing herself from her own identity. The bracketing was supposed to denote previous gender before being turned into a mutant but it was taken to mean the only valid means of identification.

Thinking about the ID itself kicked up the mental sediment at the back of her brain, and gazing over at the glass of booze she needed to settle it back down. She took the bison grass vodka and poured a shot before downing it. It burned. For a moment she felt guilt. There was no-one here to judge her for drinking in the morning anymore, except her reservations. Which burnt away. All her thoughts and anxieties on this morning dissolved within the alcohol forming a solution of happiness that soothed her mind. She let out a sigh. Her clothes were still in her green military backpack. There was a unit patch front and center, An outline of the county of Cornwall with a target and skull overhead, and a lightning bolt. She had at one point been a redeemer after all. There was also another one, it had been custom sown and the lines were a little amateurish, more so than the other. A tuxedo kitten with the words lethal kitten written over it. Then there was just a British flag, kept obscured behind the pouch was one other that she kept rather more hidden.

There had been other patches and backpacks in this place once. She looked at herself in the mirror. Her mind was still looking into the fading shadows of what once was and no longer could ever be. Her mind grasping out for reassurance, gasping. As the thoughts drew a trail of tears. She had been through it all she told herself. She had always remained. Like steel. Like cold hard steel. She looked into the mirror and would be strong. She took a shot of vodka to cast her psyche into that steel. That was the narrative she spun to defend her habit.

She took out her clothes. A black long sleeved thermal vest and DPM camouflage trousers as well as a pair of cotton thick navy-blue socks. She pulled over the heavy-set walking boots and set about tying them up. Then she was off under the sun’s bright and cool gaze. The city of London too had been hollowed out, as the grand townhouses and real-estate values had been drained white. It was a decaying giant. Limbs occasionally jerked in the throes of rigor mortis. The government trying to resuscitate the decaying urban core. Perhaps one day they would devise some technique to turn the random jerking into something more coherent. Time would one day tell. The city would continue to decay until such day. It was one of these government offices in the center she was headed towards. Past the open-air markets of Hyde Park, past the last military garrison in the Guards tower, one of the few skyscrapers that still stood tall. Whitehall and Parliament itself had been walled off. It was more of a compound occupied by the government that ruled now from Edinburgh. Still whatever operations they tried to continue in the old capital would be done in the most sterile of settings. The buildings had sustained less damage here and at one point it had served as makeshift hospital and residential area before everyone was cleared out. Those were the days, where things were supposed to go back to how they once were. Before calcifying in place as the reconstruction money ran out. As the people of London, the people of Southern England bled across Europe, leaving this corpse. One day perhaps they would be transfused back.

The walls and fences around Whitehall would come down. Guns would once more be made illegal and the monarch would return to Buckingham palace. She remembered those long dead days. The normal that had been so unassailable reduced to the ruins she walked past. She reached the gate. The guard on duty was not military. The last remains of the Peelers of all. Not that there was much civil administration south of Cambridge anymore, yet in places it still tried to live. The paperwork was easy enough. The benefit of being a Mutant, or as ever so affectionately known, a mutie, you had one of these ID cards. A double-edged sword perhaps. Having your name be registered so neatly might make you all the easier to find. For right now it served a certain utility. The next was the appointment letter all neatly laid out. He did not need to know it was a photocopy and not the technically correct watermarked copy. She was waved through.

The meeting was in Scotland Yard, the artefact investigations unit. Her employers now that her prior self-employment with her friends had fallen through. This new arrangement would have to suffice. The receptionist lived within the compound. A cushy job as a receptionist in a walled off compound wearing nice clean clothes and makeup. A tinge of envy was palpable. The receptionist looked up and noticed the interloper. Not even a regular visitor, a police officer, someone who was naturally supposed to be there. And upon the interloper’s head there were more intrusions into the receptionist’s own sense of normality as her eyes scanned over the figure before them. There were two cat ears, and a tail behind her. It was not uncommon for someone who had been to the zone to come back different mentally or physically. Even if someone in the city had heard of such things it did not temper their reaction upon first contact. It was a stare that was the mark of the interloper, she knew it so well, as the memory of a thousand such stares pierced into her.

Yet none of those memories could pierce her. She was steel. Cast through fire and flame and a shot of vodka. It would not pierce her. It could not. After all, even if her gaze did pierce there was nowhere for the shell to go that could cause any damage. For all that there was beneath that armor that could be harmed had been destroyed to the point that it could receive no further damage. There was nothing left to burn.

Once the stare had passed, she put her meeting letter on the desk with her ID card, to get the formalities out the way. The receptionist snapped out by the gestures of normality got to work tapping away at her computer. She reads out the letter and the number as if it is a name. For her purposes it is a name. A neat way of filing an interloper as something thoroughly separate. The meeting room would be the same, the artefact investigation department, basement floor one. The underworld as it was known.

“Here you go, miss- sir.” A passing shot. Even with her long hair, short stature, and other bodily features that clearly made her a woman, if it was not written in a way that she was capable of understanding bureaucratically then it did not matter. Nothing mattered other than that card. Not here in the center of the last vestige of civil administration in this former capital.

The appointment would be with DI Clark K. Mortzavi, the ostensive handler. Every so often she would go to him to receive her marching orders. Usually, a grisly murder of a police officer attempting to assert control beyond Whitehall, but it was also other interested parties. The met was not above being hired as a tool between rival artefact dealers and if in the end it made for nice reports for the suits in Edinburgh then all the better really. So long as the bribe was left absent or couched in formal language about reports from members of the public. The nominal authority and nominal laws could still be quite the potent tool. North of London their authority was less nominal and more simply normal so it really was quite the potent tool. She was thinking idle in the waiting room sitting pretty on a metal folding chair. The door opened and a couple of other muties stumbled out. This one had a lizard tail, you do not see that often. The other a wagging dog tail which was far more common, Pluto was her name, they had a history with DI Clark both sharing the same handler. Pluto was Hispanic which was uncommon and led to her being treated as even more of an interloper than otherwise.

“Hey Whiskey.” The dog tail struck up conversation. It was not the case that every mutie knew each other, far from it. However, in the same workplace and situation one does build a certain familiarity.

Whiskey returned with a nod before looking over at the lizard tail, who was unfamiliar. “Hey Pluto, who’s she? Someone I know?” Pluto gave a scowl. It was her name but not a name she liked to be called by. Whiskey knew but they went back long enough that the inertia made her unwilling to change.

“You still haven’t kicked your habit, you alcoholic whore. Are you still running with the team you had been before? Or did they all run off on you?” Pluto said tinging the final syllable with an upward inflection designed to draw as much blood as possible.

Whiskey just gave her a smile. “I’m glad you’re doing ever so well.” She said simply.

“Whatever. Enjoy Clark.” Pluto gave a wave and started walking through the exit.

The accompanying lizard tailed girl looked over at Whiskey for a moment before seeing Pluto’s exit and so she started shuffling after her. What a strange grouping she thought. The door remained closed a little longer. She took a second to look it up and down, a wooden or perhaps fake wooden door. Instead of a name plaque there was a name sticker as if made in a label maker. It was slightly lopsided.

The door clicked and swung open. Clark was not a thin man though it was hard to tell how much of that was on account of his baggy suit. He had tired dark eyes and a short beard. He held the door frame and poked his head out in Whiskey’s direction.

“Hey Whiskey, you’re up.” He said.

Her movements were sluggish as she left the chair behind and stumbled over to Clark.

“Woah, what’s going on with you here?” He looked over. “Have you been drinking?”

“So what?” Giving the best defense she could.

“Look whatever, I know you’ve been having it rough recently but.” He gave a pause trying to tabulate the best words to say without touching a nerve.

“I don’t care. What are you going to tell me, that drinking is bad, that I’m going to an early grave? Who cares. I’m already some mutated freak, and all I can do is wet work like this or rot in bed. Nobody is coming for me. Nobody cares about me. So why the hell does it matter anyway? I’m good, I can get what I have to do done, and you’re standing there pretty, you don’t have to live in a shack surrounded by the junk they all left behind.” She didn’t shout. Her words were a harsh and firm salvo but she maintained composure as she said her truth. “So yeah, I guess I am having a rough time, but I’ll get through it. That’s what they always said. Even if I’m the junk scrap steel that they all left behind.”

Clark let out a long sigh. “Okay whatever, just, get in here. You’re not that drunk anyway. You have your notebook? Right?” He looked over at her.

She tapped her pocket and it was there, a notebook that was still crusty from being wet and dried out but the pages were still in good enough state to be read and to take a pencil. Next to it in the pocket was a single HB pencil, wooden with no eraser and she probably had a sharpener in her backpack.

“So, what’s the mission boss?” Whiskey said with a smirk.

“Right straight to business then.” Clark paused before launching into it.

“Wait, actually I have a question, who was it with there with Pluto back there?”

“You’ve not met Oak yet? She’s quiet, she’s a more recent encounter actually, just a few months ago, yeah, she’s pretty good. She’s absolutely a better fit with Pluto. You weren’t good at dealing with that pup, she likes the sound of her own voice and she’s not worth disagreeing with, even though you would constantly. She still blames you for what happened with Edwin; you know, I mean I can understand why.” Clark was about to keep on speaking before he was interrupted.

“Can we not?” Whiskey let out with a sigh. The room was stuck in silence for a moment. “What about this job you have for me?”

A little taken aback by the interruption Clark started to get back on track. “Well. Where to start even. The Americans are coming here, specifically the Vice President and the Secretary of State.” Clark looked over to see Whiskey about to start writing. “I’d prefer that this information stay in the room with us here.”

“Well can’t I just write VIP? Maybe I could downgrade the country or the position? Anyway, why are they coming here and not Edinburgh? Or at least why aren’t they sticking to the bases in Cambridge?” Whiskey asked.

“It’s more that they’ve got tired of the lax approach down here. I mean I can speculate but I can’t know, I might someone who lives over here with all the comings and goings. This stuff is above my paygrade, and way above your clearance level. So, hell even if I did know I wouldn’t be telling you. As much as I love and appreciate you and your work, you mangy old cat.” He paused for a second. “Anyway look, if you have to have me tell you what I think is going on, it’s that they’re tired of dealing with London’s shit. We’re pretty close to Cambridge, where all of their expensive shit is and we’re left as this rotting urban center full of crime, full of crazy druggies, alcoholics.” He paused for a second and then gave her a grin. “No offence to you.”

“None taken.” Whiskey replied with a smile. “I guess it’s about regional stability then, so do they want a more active role? What about you? This office?”

“Look I haven’t heard anything just yet but there are rumors, murmurings, I guess. Less about a whole takeover but more oversight, they’ll be sending some of their guys here, starting to expand offices and set up more regular patrols. Bringing back normality, restoring order. That’s what I heard anyway. I don’t imagine things will change that much down here, other than perhaps bribes losing their effectiveness we’ll have to see.” Clark let out a sigh.

“Do you actually believe them? I mean they’ve been saying that for years haven’t they? About the bribes I mean.” Whiskey said with a jumbled tone as her thoughts meandered into speech.

“Well that’s the question isn’t it. You know, you’ve met some of their guys and I’ve met some of their guys and believe it more this time. Before it was some guy who’d just come down four, five hours from Edinburgh with a white paper, a nice suit, and a dream and a briefcase and not a single solitary cheque. Those guys though, they mean business.” He said with a kind of uncertain confidence.

He was not wrong though. Those guys did mean business. She had fought along side them pushing the zone back from Portsmouth. The screaming of F-35As overhead, the sound of its engines rumbling in her chest followed by the roar of the jdams falling on the line beasts ahead. When they wanted to get something done those guys did mean business.

“I guess you’re right.” She said.

“You did serve with them so I guess that makes me feel a little more confident in my own prediction. I guess what would change down here is that there might be fewer things for us to be dealing with.” Clark said upbeat.

“That is not great for me. I’m not salaried like you.” Whiskey said with a bit of concern.

“Well, there’s a cat café somewhere just outside the Green zone in Soho, I think you’d fit in there just fine. Besides it wouldn’t kill you to do something a little safer.” He said with a smirk, but his eyes showed a kind of hope.

“Wow. Well, whatever we got off topic. The job you have for me. I need more booze money after all.” She said trying to play it off and avert the tone.

“You see that’s why briefings with you are always a pain, we end up going way off topic, with Pluto and Oak, I sit her down, explain the mission, let her bitch about this that and the other for a minute, shake her hand and send her on her way. You, you find some way to make me talk about some other bullshit and here we are 15 minutes behind schedule.” With a pretend annoyed tone.

“We have fun.” Whiskey said with a smirk.

“Right, business actually this time.” Clark collected his papers for a moment to refresh his memory after the distraction. “So, we’re having this VP, well VIP, two of them they’re landing at Heathrow, now Heathrow is mostly secure nowadays but what we’re not sure about is London City, that airport should be out of commission but we haven’t got anything up to date on it, is the runway still usable? Because if it is it could lead to serious airspace threats. So we want you to have a look around, preferably just to see what’s going on down there, give us some up to date intel and avoid creating too much of a fuss if there’s anything bigger.”

“That it? Couldn’t you just send a patrol down there?” Whiskey sounded confused as she spoke and a little annoyed.

“You’re cheaper than a patrol.” He said with a laugh. “But also less conspicuous, smarter, more experienced, less corrupt, and you know what to look for. Besides they don’t want to take on the job.”

Whiskey’s thoughts lingered on the last sentence. “They don’t want to take it? Do we have any intelligence about the situation down there that leads the Americans to believe there might be a threat waiting in the wings?”

“That’s a bit above your clearance grade.” Clark lingered.

“Well? I mean if you’re sending me, I’m going to find out anyway. What’s the point in something like that? Or do the guys upstairs in the patrol rooms know the details and that’s why they won’t go, they’re worried that if anyone else hears then they’re not going to want to go?” Whiskey asked in not a fully accusatory tone but certainly in an interrogative one. “I’m fine, I’m going to take this job, I’ve got nothing better to be doing, and if you want me to end up in that cat café near Soho and not six feet under, then I need all the information you have.”

Clark let out a sigh. “Look they told me not to say, since they figure that it’s not good intel and the point is to have you scout to see what’s going on, not to get your hands dirty and actually find what they’ve heard and what they’re worried about.” He let out another sigh. “What they’ve heard upstairs is that there’s a Russian artefact dealer operating out of the airport.”

“So that’s where Medvedev ended up. I thought he was still in the yellow zone around Chelsea.” Whiskey said, semi-interrupting.

“Trust you to know.” Said Clark.

“He gave pretty good prices, specifically for cheaper artefacts too, usually beast parts, but sometimes he put in for just the pretty stuff, none of the actual useful expensive stuff, he was good to work for.” Whiskey said. “I mean he’s probably a Russian asset but.”

“He is a Russian asset. We know that one for certain. Well, they know upstairs, it’s in the report right here. That you aren’t cleared to read.” Clark sighed, as more information tumbled out of his mouth. “You’d make a really good spy you know.”

“I think you just like talking to me specifically, I’m not sure it’d work with other people.” Whiskey said with a long sigh.

“Anyway, we had intelligence that he was using the airport as his compound, and we heard that he had imprisoned a beast there himself, along with a few of his guys. Basically, the Americans don’t know but we are worried about them flying into Heathrow with the possibility of him and his guys with a runway. They want it cleared out but we’re not even sure if the intelligence is true in the first place, or if its even him and not just some other guys or if the runway is usable.” Clark asked.

“A beast huh, I guess that’s why the boys upstairs wanted to leave this to us, to the pros. I still don’t really get it though, even if the runway is usable its not like they could have a plane take off and be a threat. Certainly not enough of one to deal with a fighter escort. It’s not like they would’ve been able to fly in any fighters from Russia. Not without them or us knowing and probably not without causing an international incident.” Whiskey asked again.

“They need more artefacts for their military and like it or not we’re the only place for that. I’m not entirely sure what they’re worried about either, but my guess is decoys. Decoy drones are pretty big, and to have the kind of range they need, they’d need to be flying off from well, a runway. The idea is that they might also have some jets fly from Russia, have them loiter at long range and then launch the decoys and then strike, and with all the confusion in the airspace then they could try and play it off as a mid-air collision, and they’d have the evidence to back it up. Hell they could just do a mid-air collision anyway. It’s not like we don’t have a lot of birds in the air with all of the rebuilding the Americans are doing.” He looked over at Whiskey who still seemed a bit unconvinced.

“Well, I guess if we’re right, if the Americans are bringing things under control, it’s going to be harder for them to smuggle the artefacts they need out but this just begs the question why fly into Heathrow? Why not fly into Cambridge where there are no threats and where they have much more control?” Whiskey asked thinking things over.

“My guess is that they’ll call things off if things aren’t done, because they can’t drive here, they couldn’t fly in if City is like this and if the Russians want to go out of their way to attack then even flying into Cambridge isn’t necessarily going to be safe.” Clark said scratching his head.

“Why does this even need to happen anyway. It’s all just political pomp after all.” Whiskey said incredulously.

“That’s the point. I mean for one they don’t know if this threat is genuine, and if it isn’t there isn’t that much to worry about, a few small-time thugs and small arms, arms dealers. Even without incentive they won’t have the gear to actually do that much. I think the reason this has to happen is for us though. It’s about saving face. I don’t think the guys in Edinburgh would accept being pushed back without some gesture from them, and I think from their perspective its easier to do this than to actually do anything against the boys in Edinburgh, militarily speaking I mean. So, the strategic calculus makes sense. Also they don’t even know that there’s the possibility of them being shot down because we haven’t told them. I mean what kind of country would even humor us if we have a hostile nation operating on our own territory.” Clark said with a nod.

“I suppose.” Replied Whiskey with a sigh. “I’ll get going then, I need my gear after all.” She finished noting down the mission. She had her marching orders.

“I would have you sign the contract but we can’t really be frivolous with the photocopier, for now just sign here, on my notepad. I need a paper trail so I can pay you. I can’t just expense you anymore they’re getting all up my ass on the corruption watch shit.” Clark explained.

Whiskey sighed, walked over and took the outstretched pen writing her signature. “Are we done here?”

Clark gave a nod, and with that she was off.

Now all that would be left was the preparation for the mission. She shook Clark’s hand and then headed for the door. She had some gear lying around still. A rifle an L1A1 SLR, and a Webley revolver, but she was running low on ammunition, she would have to go shopping so to speak. The mission did not seem much trouble. Provided that she could keep to the mission anyway. Staying undetected if possible, keeping engagement to a minimum, if possible if detected, and avoiding contact with the beast, if possible, if detected and if engaged. With a spring in her step and a clear set of orders she marched for Oxford Street where the best suppliers would be.

Chapter 2

Bond Street had once been, had ceased to be, and then been reborn as a center of commerce once again. The street had finally been pedestrianized not through an act of the state but through the act of people congregating to create a large open-air market. There was anything for sale and there were no officers in sight. People would offer up a few staples, rats on skewers, if anything London today might have had fewer rats than before. With a lower population and the introduction of a hungry populace it made a degree of sense. Though then again refuse collection was a distant historic memory at this point and with the various collapsed buildings spread throughout the city there were plenty of places for rats to breed in relative safety. After all the rat trade was becoming gradually outcompeted by the return of more traditional means of nourishment. Existing as both an ultra-budget option for those still unable to buy and a delicacy for those who had developed a taste for it. Even with all the bones though the little bones were precisely why Whiskey did not prefer to eat rat. There were also squirrel skewers though those were more expensive, the premium offering as it were.

The rat stalls had lost ground to the return of the sausage roll. The meat was nondescript but well-seasoned. Perhaps the market might someday reclaim the greatness of Greggs, they were getting closer. To the point that Whiskey was tempted drawing closer. The price was reasonable but it depended on how much she had left over after buying the necessary materials for her job. The guns were not sold in the open-air section of the market. Some of the buildings flanking Oxford Street were seemingly structurally sound enough still for continued business. The weapon suppliers were in one such building. The Plaza Shopping Centre was an art deco building that had been spared much damage. It was easily one of the best retail spaces in the area and as such it is understandable that it was given to the group most able to take and hold ground. It was also slightly on the edge of the shopping area which leant it a relative seclusion and the building itself was more cavern-like than open air. As such the clientele felt more protected. There was also the fact that the building was so untouched that gave it a sense of security. It had a statue still standing over the entrance in the same place it had always been as though it had been protecting its building. Hand outstretched to the sky as though to shield the building from beasts or the bombs meant to blow them apart. There were a few stray bullet holes but those could have been more recent additions anyway from either extremely satisfied or dissatisfied customers.

Inside as Whiskey walked in there was an escalator, it was not moving but she had been told that there were plans of restarting it. It was so pristine that the stores that once occupied the place were still listed. She made her way to the first floor and the second store on the right. When she first came the stores largely ignored the old unit demarcation but as time went on, they seem to have receded into the old borders. She pushed the door open into her regular shop, Cherub Dynamics, it had a cardboard sign just above the door covering up the old branding. It depicted a small cherub holding an M4 carbine, wearing a helmet with ‘born to kill,’ and a peace button on it. Whiskey looked up and smiled at the sign and let out a satisfied sigh. Walking in the shopkeeper looked up from the rounds he was coaxing into a box and gave Whiskey a smile. It had been a while since she had crossed that threshold.

“Welcome back, good to see you man.” He gestured for Whiskey to come over. “Man, it’s been a while, how’s Sasha doing, you keeping her clean? She’s a real good one, I knew from the moment I fixed her barrel she was going to be a good one.” The shop keep spoke quickly but with a real warmth.

“I’m okay Alex, I’m good. Yeah. I guess it has been a while. Sasha’s doing good, I’m probably going to have to swing back home for her later, I have a job tonight.” Whiskey spoke slowly but maintained the same warmth.

“Night-crawling then, good job. You haven’t been shooting anything subprime through Sasha? It has really been a while you know, I wouldn’t be surprised if she was a little roughed up with substandard ammo.” Alex said pulling his hands back.

“Well, the group and I after we stopped shopping at yours, we’d go to this dealer in Chelsea. His rounds shot but having been cleaning the barrel it does look like it’s not doing too great. The receiver looks a little worn, maybe I’ll have to take her in to see you, you fixed her up the first time you can do it again.” Whiskey was not actually sure if there had been much damage or if the rate of damage was higher than it should be, but she wanted to have an excuse to come out here again. She wanted to make an excuse to come back and see him again.

“Oh, right he was that weird Russky guy wasn’t he Peter or some such? I remember him, why’d you stop going down there? His product not up to it? I mean I wouldn’t be surprised, Russkies and all that they’re all in it for the money you know, not the joy of the craft like with me.” Alex was probing a little and had unintentionally dropped a little bit of his friendliness but more because he was thinking about business now. It would be easy to think that the love of the craft line was a business strategy, but having heard Alex go on and on about rifles, calibers and seeing the length he went to, to refurbish old firearms it seemed he really did love it.

“I don’t really talk to the guys anymore. Going back to his shop didn’t feel right. Well it never really did but they liked his prices and they seemed to not like you.” Whiskey trailed off. Part of it was not wanting to talk about them anymore, part of it was that she felt guilty. She had always liked Alex but they thought he was overly friendly and one of them even called him a creep. Did not like muties like them, there were so many rumors in the group. Whiskey did not much care for them, she liked the guy even if he was a bit rough around the edges sometimes.

“I do miss you guys you know. I miss the time when you’d come down here, and I’d have something to show off to you. Even when I used to take you down to Soho and we’d shoot.” Alex started to go on.

“It’s not forever. It never was.” She said simply. “We should go shooting again, once this job is through.”

“That’d be real nice if we could. Not like old times though. I guess I could call in my crew.” Alex said.

“I still know some people. Hell I could probably get the detective out of his basement.” She said with a smile.

“Oh is that the famed DI Clark, you guys are always talking about him. I’ve always wanted to get to know the guy.” Alex said smiling too now.

“He shares my penchant for revolvers. I’m sure if you had something exotic it would pique his interest.” She was smiling, lost in the shadow of happier times for a moment. He was about to start speaking before she started up again. “Well that matter aside, I need to get to business.”

“Right yeah.” He was still smiling but far less animated. “So it was a recon mission you were doing wasn’t it?”

“Yeah, tonight I’m going to London City Airport to go and see the situation as it were. Ideally I’m going to be able to avoid direct confrontation but I want to have tools at the ready if things do go for the worse.” She was not smiling as she rattled off the technical details. “Oh I’m going to need a rope and some bolt cutters too. I lost mine so if you could. I can afford them and everything, just if you have some.”

“You’ll want some more 7.62x51 NATO for Sasha I take it. You still have magazines for it or need a couple more? Any kind of rounds you’re wanting in specific.” He asked.

“Oh that’s right.” She paused for a second. “Can I ask that the next thing I say stays between us?” She subconsciously hushed her voice and moved slightly across the counter towards him.

“Sure man. I don’t have any reason to go round telling.” He leaned over the counter as well in a sign of agreement.

“From what I’ve heard they’re holding a beast at the airport.” Whiskey said quietly.

“A beast huh? It’s been a while since one of those was in London. Why held up though?” Alex asked.

Whiskey shrugged. “It’s what I’ve heard, but in light of that I wouldn’t mind a box of incendiary, and a box of FMJ. If you could. Oh and binoculars, I broke my last pair. And the bolt cutters. And the rope.”

Alex nodded as he started walking round back. Whiskey laid her hand on the counter, tapping her fingers away, looking around the store. He was back promptly. He was holding in one hand a couple of ammo boxes, and in the other a canvass bag. Whiskey had assumed it was the other paraphernalia she needed, but his smile led her to believe it was something else.

“I forgot, I just got this in, this’d be perfect for you tonight I mean.” He set the boxes on the counter along with the canvass from which he pulled a strange looking pistol. It was obscure, old and arcane, and exceedingly rare. It was a Welrod pistol, in strikingly good condition. It’s shots were subsonic and thus made significantly less noise than other comparable firearms, though at the cost of range. Even more interestingly was that it shot standard ammo, rather its construction led to the shots being subsonic when shot. A nifty feat of second world war engineering.

“Wow.” Whiskey said with a smile.

Alex smiled back “Yeah.” He said simply.

“There’s no way I can take it though. Even if the ammo’s relatively cheap, this belongs in a museum. I probably can’t afford it.” Whiskey said scratching the back of her neck.

“Just take it for tonight. We can call it a test. Or a rental. Pay me back when you get paid.” Alex said happily.

“I can’t. It’s such a good gun it’d be wasted on me.” Whiskey said again. Though this was less of an impassioned rejection and more a rejection to ensure he was sure about passing on such a relic.

“Just bring it back to me okay?” He said as he pushed it towards her. “You probably shouldn’t be firing this at all until you mean it, to keep the wipes intact.” He started pulling on the bolt. “This is the bolt action see?” He handed it to her and she went through the motion.

“If you’re giving it to me then I’ll take it. I’ll bring it back to you though probably not in perfect condition.” Whiskey said with a smile and a sigh.

“That’s what I wanted to hear.” Alex replied.

Alex went and got the rest of what she needed and she exchanged paper money with him. They still used the old pre-war bank notes after all. She gave him a wave and went on her way. On her way out she saw Pluto and Oak she gave them a nod but it seemed Pluto was still a little annoyed at her.

She decided to walk her way back to Kensington, to the townhouse which had been drained of life. She was the sole and remaining inheritor of the building. Carefully she loaded magazines. Two full metal jacket, two incendiary. She put on her webbing and put the full metal jacket on her left and the incendiary on her right. She did not load the rifle immediately. She packed a torch, the other things she had got from Alex in her back pack. Along with the Welrod instead of her usual revolver. Then she rested for a moment.

Once again, the call of the booze came over her. Her memories of the house when it was full of life. When there were other glasses half full of liquor. When she drank to make the good times feel better, rather than to just cleanse herself of any bad thoughts. When there were other people to cheers with. A burned up past, burnt to ashes, burnt like the back of her throat as she drank, burning the feelings she had into ash. Before a mission she needed something to steady her hand. It was going to be a long walk in the dark and the cold. She needed the warmth to ward off the cold. Using that rationale, she had one more shot. Now that she had something to drink, she set about getting something to eat as well. As a group at one point, they bought ration packs in bulk that curiously went forgotten. Her alimony payment paid in pre-preserved penne alla arabiata. It did not taste bad. She poured water into the retort pouch and listened to it heat and steam. She held the steam in her face for a moment. It was nice and relaxing. Her ears twitched a little at the sudden hot air. The food and booze were a welcome reprieve.

She looked at the newspaper on the floor. It was an issue from a few months ago. People had started selling papers fairly recently after the liberation and at first, she was an avid reader. She was not sure when she stopped wanting to pay attention to the news. The newspapers went read by other people in the group but she started just going from day to day. It did not matter to her anymore. She was happy with the people she had. The state of the containment of the war itself was no longer so much of a worry of hers. Even having fought in it and worked in its aftermath it just did not feel like it mattered as much as the touch and love of the people around her. Even as those people passed out of her life she did not feel the need to once again find a sense of solace in the papers. Perhaps she should pick it up again. Something to ease her mind, more than booze and food.

It had been a few weeks but it was only now that she really set about going through the scant possessions that had been left strewn in the rooms that had at one point housed more than just her. A body with its organs scraped out, yet still remained the collective tissue, veins and arteries headed to nowhere. The punctuation around the redacted words giving the shape of absence. She was the only one in this place who could still remember where everything was supposed to belong. The miasma of memories growing thicker with every second she remained in this place. She could see the happier times, she could see the scenes of happier times. As if she could just reach out and carry on where everything was left off.

She left the complex behind. It would take two maybe even three hours of walking to reach the remains of London city airport from Kensington by foot. Her calves were thick and strong but even she would need to take breaks on such a long sojourn across the city. She walked through the ruined city, hearing the symphony of civilization increase in volume with every step. She was to step through the whole performance. It was shorter than before. The buildings now monuments to that longer fuller symphony that had been lost to time. As she passed through Knightsbridge she saw a curious sign for the London Underground station. It was one of the underlying bass lines that had been destroyed. Some still used the tunnels, others did not dare. Fear that the beasts still stirred down in the depths. To be fair to them the last beast was indeed found in those tunnels. It was one of the bigger ones too.

Sometimes the small worms would pop up, and they were a problem if they infested and animated something after the fact, but by and large they were manageable. Beasts are never fun to encounter. In the end they shot it. No, they blew it up. Putting an end to the cacophony of the beasts which had in the first place chased away the London underground’s bassline. Chased out the entire symphony for a time. Replacing it with their own din.

As she neared Buck’s palace she could see the imposing walled city of Whitehall. Searchlights gazed from on high next to the wall. Keeping a watch on their composition. Ironically the rest of the notes shrunk into an interlude as one approached its border. This would be no place rest not even for a moment. She knew her destination already. She could see it a note on the page as the composition regained some density and body. It was once a bar and now it was a bar again. The note coming to occupy the same place on the music sheet even if it was made by a different set of hands. She walked in to Evans holding court in the Deadenders, her drinking establishment.

The bar was filled with swirling smoke and it seemed both livelier but more militarized than usual. There were the dpm and berets of average British squaddies but then other uniforms. It was the Americans. They had strayed far from their hunting grounds. There were two green zones in London nowadays, the walled city of Whitehall and the Embassy grounds south of the river. The original US embassy had been long since destroyed but the Battersea tower still stood and so that is the place they occupied, fortified, and expanded. They came to occupy and retrofit the Battersea PowerStation once again into a power station of their own. What is a symbol of American power without its own power grid. Evans was busy dealing with the new clientele regaling them about one story or another but still she spared Whiskey a nod as she entered. The story she was telling was likely not one she was herself involved with but one she heard from a patron and now wore as her own. Whiskey took a seat in a secluded booth. Her ears twitched towards the sound teasing the words out from the background noise.

Evans was surrounded by the soldiers as she continued her story, “we were together, fighting the final beast of London, in the tunnels of the old London Underground, the metro system here, there were twenty different sets of leg arranged haphazardly around the body, and the body itself was as large as the trains that used to go around the depths. It had so many teeth and mouths on every part of it. No, it was bigger, it was bigger than the tunnels, it was so big that it left cracks behind in the tunnels it was so big, mounds of flesh, growing out and out. Anyway, we filled it full of incendiary, good old incendiary .556 like you guys use and after an eternity it finally died. So, did you guys see any action like that today? No way that you did.”

Whiskey stepped over at the break in the story, “It was not twenty sets of legs, it was thirty and you could hear the sounds of the bones breaking in each leg and regrowing as it moved around the tunnels. The scariest thing was not the bone noises, not even the smell. That’s what Evans told me anyway.” Whiskey said to expand the mythology as she patted Evans on the shoulder and gave her a grin.

“That’s right! It was so big!” Evans said trying to continue the embellishment.

Whiskey toyed with the idea of piling on more details making it even more discordant with the reality of that night until it became unbelievable. Though for new squaddies and new Americans perhaps they would not understand where the boundary between reality and fiction were in things related to the zone. It was after all a place where history and myth intersected into an indiscernible miasma. Most of all she wanted service though and a free drink there was no way she was going to take on the Russians sober. Not without a drink and a smoke in fact and there was no other bar where she could entertain the notion of drinking for free.

“That beast was something else. You remember, you were there weren’t you Evans? I wish I could have been there. To see how the mission went.” Whiskey placed more emphasis on every word. Trying to spell out that if she did not get Evans’ attention then the jig would be up. Besides as much as Whiskey did love Evans dearly, she was not about to let her tell that story without trying to put a bit of distance between her and the persons involved. It was not an easy night.

“Yeah, it was, anyway guys, have a good night, hope you get some stories like that to tell while you’re here.” Evans lifted her glass up and took a small sip as the soldiers following her spirit but not her actions took gulps of their beer.

As Whiskey and Evans walked to that small secluded booth Evans gave her a glare. “I was just getting the business to roll in, not like you care, always drinking off of my tab.”

“I wouldn’t have cared if you weren’t trying to pass off my story as yours.” Whiskey gave a serious look.

Evans gave out a sigh. “I’m sorry, look I didn’t know that you were going to come tonight, but its nice to see you. Those guys are nice but it takes it out of you to talk to them all you know. Keep the energy up. I guess it’s nice to have a break, besides he can always step in.” She gestured her head towards the other bar tender, her business partner a male mutie with a monkey tail. “It’s a good time for Jack to go in and get some experience with customers anyway. The freak show might be nice for them.”

“Why are they here, so many of them anyway?” Whiskey knew the answer but it was always safer to act like you knew less than you knew and bait other people into revealing what they knew. Besides Evans was not by any stretch of the imagination bound by any laws. Not that Whiskey really was either but it would be worse for her business working with the state than it would be for Evans. If news made its way to a barkeep then it was beyond any reasonable means of enforcement.

“They’re trying to make a new green zone, nearer to the middle of town, just a small one but in that old embassy of theirs the one with the gold eagle. They were going around cleaning it out apparently.” Evans said with no reservations.

“Long way from Battersea, or Cambridge aren’t they?” Whiskey asked but fumbled the delivery.

“Look I know you’re probing me for information, on a job are we? Or just looking for military secrets for fun?” Evans shot back. “I’m your friend, I’m sorry about earlier but you don’t need to bullshit me to get me to tell you info.”

“Sorry. Look. Today. This job is going to be fine I know it is. My calves hurt a bit the job, it’s out by London city airport, it’s quite far so I’m just taking some time to relax here before moving onto the next leg. I need a drink, and normally it wouldn’t bother me you telling my stories. It’s just today I guess, I keep on thinking back to that night.” Whiskey said in a different and decidedly earnest tone compared to before.

“I get it. Your usual today then? Whiskey on the rocks, your namesake?” Evans got up and paused. “You have your lighter?” Whiskey held up her shiny metal zippo. “Your cigarettes of choice would be menthols right?” Whiskey did not actually like Menthols but Evans did and if that’s what her friend was smoking then she would indulge too.

Whiskey nodded and Evans got out of the booth and set about preparing everything. Jack was a strange name for a mutie, but maybe nowadays people were okay with that. Fancy that. Thinking about us and seeing us as regular people same as anyone or anything else. People with regular names. Regular wants and dreams. People who want to be treated regularly. She needed that drink and that cigarette. To dissolve that thought pattern in alcohol. Burn it away with the smoking cigarette. Stub it out. Evans was quick in bringing over the necessary medicine and the application was equally quick.

“I’ve always wondered why you’d ask for it on the rocks if you were just going to shotgun it like that.” Evans asked.

“I just needed it.” Whiskey said.

“Well let me get you another few then.” Evans said getting up.

Whiskey took in that cigarette taking a long drag. She could hear the sound of the flame burning as she breathed in. Evans came back with her drinks. Whiskey looked into her eyes and said two words “Thank you.” She meant them the most of any words she had said that day. They were also the last words she remembered saying that day.

Chapter 3

The final stage of the journey would involve another rest stop in the last place the symphony could still be heard in full, the last place to gain a rest more wholesome than squatting in some dilapidated room where your company could be a rat, a dead body or another squatter. The only people who lived past the old center of the city where those unable to. There were isolated communities all dancing to their own tune but few of them in the doldrums of the old suburbs. What civilization there was clung to the riverside. As the yellow zone gave away to the orange zone. Except Liverpool Street, the gateway to the remains of London. The mouth through which the body could eat whatever vagrants had journeyed their way towards the city. It was not the only port of entry. The city boundary was fuzzy, and there was no real central authority to distinguish the line. Liverpool street was just tailored towards it. Well connected by roads that had been cleared during the reclamation effort. The road between Liverpool Street and Cambridge stood as one of the few motorways which was not still clogged by twisted metal. While other routes were available it was the path of least resistance to cling to the roads. Still surfaced, on occasion still used officially. The checkpoints largely tapered out a few miles out of Cambridge but the clear road remained. What entered the city over land entered through Liverpool Street.

This was the place where she took her rest. Nothing fancy, a tea at a cheap stall. It was warm and not much else. It was too expensive for what it was. There was no escaping it, she needed it. She lingered around the stalls and the old benches where the various dregs of society accumulated. The edge of civilization meant it was a popular waystation. There were comings and goings. Though it inhabited the large station concourse it did not use it. The rails stood ready and waiting to accept trains that would never come. Up the line there was one train that lay forlorn on its side. It had been converted into the cheapest travelling accommodation in the vicinity. A sleeping bag on a derailed train. The owner would give you cigarettes for free if he liked you though. That would have been an incentive for Whiskey to draw near, were she still in the owner’s good graces. As such she tried to find anywhere else that was cheap. She did have her own sleeping bag, but leaving the warm hubbub of civilization gave her a sense of pause.

She needed her wits about her, her senses sharpened, she needed her memory to plan. It was not as though she could not go into a darker deeper part of town after a night out. She had done so before, but the key difference being part of a group, part of a team. To go out to forgo sleep and rest is like trying to keep a pencil that is constantly writing sharp, it is bound to burn down into the pencil. Her longest record without sleep was during the Portsmouth reclamation. Forty-two hours, rolled into a British army unit. All she need do was keep her eyes open, keep her senses going, letting the memories meld and bleed into each other. Any other time drifting away from her. Only here, now. Listening to orders. Listening for beasts. Looking for any movement anything out of the ordinary. Until the cavalry came to rescue them. Operating alone and a few years older she could not afford such a degraded sensory environment. Not when she was master and commander of her own operation. She was to scout sure, but scouting required only the sharpest senses.

It was such a set of conclusions that paved her way towards the Upper Crust. She was not familiar with the patron of this establishment but gave the man standing outside with a gun a nod. An armed man outside usually did enough to deter would be criminals considering there were much easier pickings from those without the money to buy into somewhere to sleep. Even if the reward was greater, the slight risk was infinitely larger than little to no risk. Behind some crudely constructed blackout curtains was a cashier and a sign with prices. After handing over the amount of money which was a little more expensive than she had hoped she took out her sleeping bag and settled down in the bottom left corner. She had instinctively moved toward the bottom right but found it already occupied. As far from the door as possible. She fell asleep quickly and woke up suddenly but rested enough. It was now dark outside. She nodded to the guard and carried on her way.

First, she made her way to whatever remained of Tower Gateway station and along whatever remained of the DLR. Discerning eyes could trace the tracks, the great causeway that once wandered its way down the city. She was careful not to be led astray by false tracks, by collapsed old buildings that in their undeath could resemble the bones of the old DLR. She took in the night, feeling the wind, the cold nipping at the tips of her ears, the new and different ears she had now. Letting them twitch, letting them create a soundscape of our environment.
