She could hear the worms burrowing. Her eyes could catch glimpses of them in the rubble. There were no colonies of them so far, no clumps together, holding buildings in their legion grasp. Though if they ever got that big there would be a job for it. Instead, there was shuffling in the rubble. She could hear the high-pitched song of their electric dance. The siren song of the orange zone.

A dancing piece of rubble made its way across her path. The blue luminous lines animating the decaying urban bones. She readied her rifle, her Sasha, loaded with incendiary. Eyes fixed, hands steady but still quivering. Her breath hung in the air blocking her sight. Were the worms not bright and glowing she could not see them, but even so it would be hard to miss a dancing concrete slab. She pulled down the trigger, sending each shot individually. There was a brilliant flash of fire at the foot of the slab. The smoke and fire as she fired again, and again. Then silence. The electric hum having been banished. The slab stood still. The bones stayed dead. It was an indulgence. Would have been better to just ignore, to evade. The sound of gunfire was distinct and it may attract attention. Not to mention the rounds she used may be useful later. She moved on quickly. Not jogging but at the same time not slowly. Still, she still had it, her shots landed on target. The foe was felled. She needed to know that she still had it in her.

She continued her march onwards and once she was apart from the place she felled the worms she repacked her magazine. Though this time with simple full metal jacket rounds. They had the names of unknown Russian soldiers already written on them; she could read the invisible death certificates as she packed them in. She could only hope that there were no such rounds with her name on them. Though they could not kill her. She was steel. She could not be felled so easily. It began to rain as it often did, drizzle as it was once called. She pulled a hood over her head.

The area around London city airport was less decayed, there were two towers still standing. The smell of sugar still hung in the air from the long-destroyed sugar factory. As though it still clung to the air. Even with the rain. The memory of what this place once was. She had to be careful now. There were floodlights where the airport contacted the mainland. Looks like someone had occupied the airport after all, and it was likely the Russians. There was commotion, the roads were clear. The hum of civilization all the more disquieting for how silent it should be. It made her skin crawl. Usually, she was soothed but here it felt somehow unnatural, as though the symphony was conducted with rigidity and the instruments played by puppets. There were three towers still standing somehow south of the runway. Slowly she made her way around. There were patrols of soldiers making their rounds around a supposedly quiet sector. She dodged them, stepping quietly until she reached the first tower. It did not appear occupied, except for the worms. Perhaps they were the ones keeping it upright. The sound the hum growing stronger and stronger as she made her way. She could not see them. Not in those densely packed corridors.

Flat by flat, floor by floor. Worm infested though it may be she could tell a good vantage point when she saw it. One of the flats the door stood open. It had decayed in its years of emptiness yet a mug still stood alone on a table. She walked towards it. The sports direct branding still visible. The tea had been rendered to a putrid mulch by the years. Still, whoever last crossed this threshold, other than the newly wormy occupants might have expected to return, leaving their tea half drunk. Did they live, did they die. There was nothing that the old room could tell. It was a means of wasting time as she gathered the volition to continue up and up. Then she stopped. There was a sound. It clawed into the back of her mind. Now she knew why this was not used as a vantage point by the soldiers. There was the faint high-pitched whine up there. Well either the Russians were sloppy, or it was a recent move on their account, or that which made the noise was a new occupier. Instead, she made her way down the staircase. She had not been thinking about the possibility of a patrol. Just because she thought her sounds were masked by worms did not mean they were. For the high-pitched sound was not within the range of hearing for everyone. An adjustment she had yet to get used to.

“остановка,” was the single shrill word that came from the soldier’s mouth, as she felt the warmth of a flashlight on her.

A request she was not meaning to honor. Normally she would flee upwards where she had the advantage, but she was not going to risk going up above. Instead, she darted forward. Jumping her calves bending as she dived for cover. Instead of the hail of bullets they tried to parley with her. Underpaid conscripts sent out on night orders, they do not want to fight or die. Shame they ran into her. She gripped the Welrod, holding the wire grip. As if the steel could give her the resolve. It had to be done. There was nothing else for it she told herself. She darted forward. Before the impulse to shoot, before he even registered the sight of the girl with cat ears in front of him there was a bullet in his brain. Putting a hard sharp stop to any action. The sound was remarkably quiet to the point that to the other soldiers the fact that their comrade had been shot had not been registered. A fatal mistake for the second soldier who would soon find another bullet planted in his brain. The third and final soldier made the fatal mistake of reaching for his radio and not his weapon. Perhaps he just sought the comfort of authority as the situation his brain flailed for anything to comfort him in the situation he struggled to register. Mistakes are punished by death.

She could smell the blood. The sweet sickly metallic smell. At least she could not taste it. At least it was their blood and not hers. There was nothing else for it. She examined the bodies. There was nothing of interest except a single satchel charge held by the one who tried to use the radio. She deliberately avoided looking at their IDs. Instead searching for anything else. The radio would be a problem but if she twisted the top then interference could be blamed on incompetence. A broken radio was more believable than an open radio. She took the satchel charge, the fuse. It was so old fashioned. Clearly, they were not sending their best, or perhaps they did not think it necessary to send their best. Either way it led to the same result and either way whatever was left of the Royal Army was being chewed up maintaining the Bristol wall, and the Americans were too busy pratting about on embassy expeditions to find themselves here. If it could be done cheaply then it did not really matter either way.

There were the makings of a plan in her head. Scouting mission this may have been till she killed three conscripts. Conscripts do not just die. She considered feeding them to the worms. Though the animated meat strewn together by worms would eventually be found and examined. No, she wanted to do more. She wanted to prove to good old DI that she still had it. It was the pride and hubris in her heart that drove her forward. Closer and closer to the bottom of the building. The supports of the building in the basement. That is where the sound was the strongest. There they were, thousands of them filling the supports, unable to move anywhere but up pinned in place by the pressure of that building. To say nothing of what was in the God’s above calling out. The whimpering. They were both tied together. Still. If one support were to be severed the whole building would likely be felled and what was laid to rest at the top would be flung into an encampment of Russian troops. The best-case scenario for them would be heavy losses, at worse the entire contingent would be wiped out. Mission accomplished.

Now she had to make sure she took out the right support, so the building collapsed just so to land on the airport. Perhaps it would be the final landing that air strip would ever receive. She took out a compass. If she blew the closest pillar the imbalance combined with the worms would propel the tower into an inevitable collision course with the ground. If only she had some devil paste to soothe the friction. She had sold her last cannister a long time ago for booze. None of the artefacts she once had ended up surviving her drunken selling spree to facilitate further drinking. It was intended as a farewell party in a manner of speaking. Still no point dwelling on it. She stopped half way through turning back. Never to be the same again.

She took the charge, and set the fuse carefully before pressing it into the charge. It was ten minutes. Ten minutes to head towards the airport entrance. Ten minutes. There were names now. Men who breathed who would not for the machinations of a timer that ticked away far from their earshot. Ten minutes. Every second hung as an eternity. She rushed towards the most fortified place. Knowing that the garrison would be taken surprised and pressed into rapid service to contend with what lay ahead.

Whiskey remembered the first time she had to contend with the Green Knight, the faint sound of the air bumping against a thousand blades. Thinner than glass. A slight distortion in the air. A thousand blades, a thousand threads, a thousand teeth, for a thousand mouths. Dangerous to walk through, not compatible with survival when thrown at high enough velocity to disrupt the anomaly. Leaving a trail of beheaded corpses. They were soldiers. They were here in a foreign country occupying land that was not theirs. Who cares if they became beheaded corpses. Who cares if they left friends and widows behind. It was war. The only reason they hesitated to do the same to her was lack of experience, lack of backbone. Anxiety, cowardice, and hesitation were lethal. You learned or you died. Those soldiers would never get the same chance to learn. Just like so many comrades lost to anomalies. That’s life. C’est la vie.

Ten minutes. She assumed a position behind cover. There were several guards smoking, some standing behind machine guns. The fuses counted down to the deaths. She did not know what was on the runway. Who were in the path of all those silent blades. There was a far-off crack as the charge finally broke. A tremendous crash. The building proved woefully inadequate at reaching the runway. The furthest any of it got was clattering against the concrete wall, with some scattered parts of it reaching the water. It appears she underestimated the distance and height. Still the air was filled with the humming of a thousand worms now freed, the pressure bursting over everywhere such that there was a wriggling line of blue in the sky.

It was not what she intended, far less lethal, but it led to a commotion in the airport as the guards stood to attention. There would have to be some recon. Then the most peculiar thing happened. There was the sound of an explosion. A secondary coming from the airport. Followed by another and then a larger explosion. There was screaming. Then the worms started to limber up. As they sowed together the bones of the building and followed the warmth of burning fire and explosives. The front of the airport was in chaos. There was only a single guard there. The cigarette had long since left his lips as he gripped his machine gun.

What was going on behind his eyes? There was probably little more than the animalistic fear pulsing through the shaking trigger finger. Clinging to that which he thought would provide him salvation and safety. The sound of chaos, death and gunfire continued behind him. The round that passed through his neck answered the fear drenched question in the back of his mind. He would be next. Whiskey’s FAL, her Sasha smoked from the muzzle. Then she watched and waited for a stir. To see if any of his comrades would notice, if they would come to take his place. After she sat listening to the far-off screaming, the crack of gunfire, the humming in the air of a thousand worms acting together. Sitting there behind rubble listening to the night. To the final screams of men. Only listening. Trying not to think to stifle and drown any guilt any thoughts that those screams were caused by her.

Once she was quite sure the coast was clear, Sasha was stowed away, it was the Welrod’s time to shine. There was a stink in the air. The screaming had started to die down. There was only so many lungs to scream. There was still the hum in the air. Then a sudden thud. The ground thundered with the sudden shockwave. The hum ceased. As a thousand tiny lives spluttered out into nothing. Silence crept in. It was time for the survivors to huddle together. To take time to make sense of the minutes of carnage, minutes that would change the years, the decades of countless people. It would not matter. It was not over. There were still bullets left bound for the cerebral cords of men loaded into her Welrod. The power had gone out and no-one had sought to intervene. There was the smell of rust. Of men bleeding.

Whiskey hung to the shadows. There was a commotion of words in a foreign language. Screams interspersed with words. They seemed to be flooding into the lobby filling it as a makeshift CASEVAC area. The smell of antiseptic filled the air. There was something else too. It was sweeter than sweet, sweeter than the sugar from the long dead factory. It sent a chill down her spine. Devil paste. She tried to tease the nature of what was going on, she saw a doctor, or perhaps a soldier opening a canister and fill a syringe. Then use it to seal wounds. Trying to maintain a thin enough film so that it created a seal but not so much that the two pieces of flesh would be constantly sliding against each other. It was not unknown to any redeemers. Devil’s paste was a miracle in a bottle, but it was just as common to know someone who had screwed it up. It had to be thinner than anything. Still, it might hold wounds for a while. Still, it worked best against the fine blades of the green knight, less good against the wounds caused by shrapnel and explosions, but anyone who had been through that anomaly deserved treatment first. Otherwise, they would slowly bleed out.

Whiskey crept forward. The beast wherever it was seemed to be contained still. They would know if it was not. The screams served to drown out her footsteps incredibly effectively. The men whose screams came by her hand at least peripherally. Past the entrance foyer there was a strong iron door where the guts of the baggage system once wormed their way into the logistics chain. Past deprecated check in desks for an airport that was now dead and buried. There was the hum of a generator, the red light of a backup generator. More alive than the bodies outside in the makeshift clinic. As the ranks of the dead grew and grew step by step.

There was a heavy-set door right there. This had to be it. The door had a nice lock on it, it would have taken some time to open if it were not already open, beckoning her beyond. On the other side was a laboratory, several specimens dancing in solution. There were beast body parts, the smell of devil paste, and so many other priceless artefacts besides. So many lifetimes of redeemers, so much blood congealed and coagulated into this dusty room. The beast had to be somewhere but this room seemed much too small to contain one unless it was some form of aberrant. She had no need of a flashlight clarified senses as she looked out into the dark, the red light of the backup generators.

As she made her way through the entrails of the lab she heard a clang, first one then another. It was not getting closer instead it sat there. Sound. Bang bang bang bang. Over and over. She stepped towards it. There was a great chamber that reeked. The smell of shit and pennies. Of dead bodies. It was made more intense by the ventilation system that was running rougher on the backup power. In the middle there was meat. Only meat, and as she stepped into this chamber the floor felt warm through her boot. Unusually so. It was wet. Like the floor was sweating. The floor tensed and as it did the film of flesh bounced against the floor making rhythmic bangs. Something was alive. The flesh seemed to tense up like a tarpaulin around a central point. It was a metal sarcophagus with the meat stretching out from its rusted edges. In, out, and held in place by the lid. Whatever it was lay behind it. It had to be the beast. She regretted not taking explosives. Walking back, she found a view onto the flesh where she was not stepping on it, but she was also able to shoot at it. Loading incendiary, she waited for the flesh to tense up took in a breath and tightened her finger on the trigger. The typical smoke trail, then crack, then flash rang out in the room. It hurt a lot particularly in such a confined space the sound bouncing around. The banging ceased.

She fired a second and third shot for good measure. The film stopped moving and black blood started leaking from it like from a soy sauce packet. Then it contracted the black liquid pushing out and through the corridor, it was warm and sticky. It smelled like sap and fat, as oil congealed on top of it. The clanging gave way to the sound of grinding as the sarcophagus started to move pushing open. A hissing of something. It was human shaped as it came out of its box. Whiskey pulled up her rifle again and this time fired again at the target the bullets cracked and burned. They pierced straight through the center of mass. It turned towards her, as it started to inflate a film around it. It lifted up into the air and let out a scream. It started to crackle and move up and down the register. This was something else. She knew the sound a threshold was opening up right here.

There was a commotion as the Russians whichever ones were still able to move started to thunder up the passageway. They probably would have taken issue with Whiskey had they noticed her, but there was a much more pressing issue at hand. There were now several muzzle flashes joining her and the bullets started to be distorted. Trajectories bending with the sound and piercing into the group of soldiers. They kept on shooting all the way until the end. She knew it was coming. They knew it was coming. A stirring in her guts with the sound. Slowly coming to anticipate it. The thunderous crack as the room started to fray at the seams. The room started to fold into itself. A meaningless sea of metal and light and matter. Of atoms and quarks. Crack, crack, crack. Scream, scream, scream. Light, light, light. Guts, guts, guts. What was a person in this sea of meaningless signals. The chaos of the universe finally embodied and encapsulated in a confined space. She let go of her rifle. Clatter, clatter, clatter. Bang, bang, bang. Flash, flash, flash.

Chapter 4

This was her fourth time here. In this white room. No, her fifth time. Three times from without, twice from within. The final light flashing through her visual cortex. It should have been the swansong of life. Maybe for someone else their life would be flashing in front of them. Every memory, every good day, every smile. Everything that made a life worth remembering. Perhaps it was just so that she was given respite from the recollection. A burned-out husk of slag pounded over and over and over and over. Dents inflicted from within and without. Nothing better for a pig iron plate as herself. One that kept on inserting itself into every single application. Never up to the task, but always wanting to interrupt. Sent out on a mission to put the runway out of commission and now all of this. It was just what she deserved. This white room. It was going to start dissipating now, as the memory of what light was left her mind. The inky blackness started to fill her up. There was no boundary anymore, no light to distinguish. There was only the inky dark. It was familiar now. Not that she welcomed it or could welcome it. Or that it would care if she did or did not. It was all that there was anymore. All that would be.

The sensation of waking back up was always one of excruciating pain. The body began to reset itself. The muscles and tendons snapping back into place painstakingly stitching themselves back together. Her body that had dissolved was reconstituting itself and she could feel every part of the sensation. All the pressure pressing down on her. The wetness of the blood she lost. The wounds that made them slowly reversing and falling back into place. Her head stung worse than cigarette withdrawals like part of her mind was so preoccupied with the pain it was unable to fully rebuild itself. The pain occupying so much of it that was all she could think of. It was worse than a hangover. It was even worse than usual. The injuries that charted the course of death more extensive than they had been before. Recovering from a fatal gunshot wound was usually simple. The brain would start to fall back into place and there were not any pain receptors there. The skull was trickier and a little more painful but usually pretty quick. There were fewer muscles that needed to be set into place again. This time it was everywhere. It was so everywhere and always.

She tried to open her eyes but her mind had not been configured for that quite yet. She did not know if she was opening her eyes, if her brain had the capability to process images just yet. Instead, there was blackness. She tried to move her arm but there was no movement or sensation. She could feel something in her legs. The burning pain of the calves now growing back on the bone nestling themselves in the right places to facilitate movement. She just lay there assumedly for a moment. It might have been silent it might not have been. All she could do was wait to be washed back up from that inky black shore. Bit by bit, pain and faculties returning. Get up. Get up. Get up. Get up. Get up.

When she started to come to, she could see the husk of the metal room. It had been blown apart sometimes with straight lines sometimes with curved lines or cracks. The deconstruction of the antechamber was total and she could feel the wind on her face and the cold morning air. The sunrise had come and gone at this point. There was the smell of shit and pennies from the dead soldiers around her. She was the solitary survivor of sorts. Getting up was a struggle. The pain still jagged and still pressing into her. She was lucky that there had been some kind of blast that kept her clear of the rubble. The thought of being forced to live and then die and be resurrected in excruciating pain four more times was not exactly something she was enamored with. Though it would at least be the end of all this. It was a little tempting but not for today.

To her surprise there was no threshold even if she had thought there was one opening up. Instead there was the rather elementary scene of a room blown apart albeit in pretty creative ways. It was more like a modern art exhibit than something truly otherworldly. Her uniform was totaled. Not totaled exactly but full of holes to the point it compromised the insulative efficiency of it. That was to say she felt cold. She limped back towards the way she came. There was the smell and the silence. There was not a single soul left alive. For a moment she wondered if there was a soul left in London. The silence giving her a disquieting answer for now. It was not terribly likely that it was all over but when she needed the reassurance of life there was nothing terribly forthcoming. As she approached the makeshift medical tent she saw a scene of death. There was nothing left. Nobody left. It looked quick at least, rubble crushing much of it.

She made her way towards anywhere less bombed out trying to find a barracks. She needed something new to wear even if she did not terribly want to wear a Russian uniform it was not as though other options were terribly forthcoming. As she walked the length of the bombed-out terminal eventually she found an area that backed onto some tents. The tents too were full of holes but this appeared to be where the men slept. She looked in the footlocker and found a uniform. It was not well made and did not have any lining but it was something she could wear. Instead of stripping she opted to take the oversized clothes and wear them atop of her tattered uniform. She left it all behind. Sasha was nowhere to be found and the Webley was gone. That would be an annoying apology to make. It would be annoying to rearm in the first place. Just as annoying as rearming itself as her arm continued to scream.

Where from now. Where to go. She could only walk. Try to find signs of life. Then a flash of an idea in her head. The Green Zone it had to be the Green Zone. The office with that detective in it. It would be a bit of a walk. On unsteady feet as she walked like a newborn foal. Her mind filled with nothing than the static of motion. Each foot in front of the other over and over. Over and over. Over and over. Foot in boots. Boots in feet. Feet in steps. Steps in feet. Boots in steps. Steps in boots. Holes in boots. There were holes in her boots. She had not even thought about it yet but now as her feet started reforming themselves and the synapses clicked back into place, she could feel the cold morning breeze conducted through her feet. Cold, cold, cold. Step, step, step. The journey continued. She did not know the way in her mind but she knew it in her feet guiding herself along the river in a contraflow. Stepping against the current of her body. The cold and the pain searing into her. Step after step. Limping now. Her left leg dragged against her trying to go with the current. Still, she kept on stepping. It would be hours. In a dazed state she made her way. Foot, foot, foot, foot. Forward, forward, forward. River, river, river.

It hurt. It hurt. It would not, not hurt. Her mind imprisoned in such a way that she could never imagine it not hurting. Yet still she carried on. It was far from one foot in front of the other and far more of a limping motion with one better more composed leg slowly being guided forward press by press by the other leg. Something she was conscious of. Trying to maneuver her foot and her leg in the best way to minimize pain. Moving her arms in the best way to minimize agony. The pain pressing into her like an obstacle course that only she could feel.

The green zone was a hive of activity as trucks came and went. The wall had been blown in places and all of the buildings had been struck by rocks and stones coming from an easterly direction. Like an earthquake the eerie jagged wounds on the buildings. There were blast marks around the doors and signs of breaking and entering. It was hard to tell where the natural disaster ended and the human disaster began. There were people in cuffs being lined up outside the buildings and soldiers all around. Inside the medley of men was DI Mortazavi. He had a bloody nose and had been cursing all morning. Cursing after the mangy cat who was limping her way towards him. The situation was stable and the only people bleeding or with blankets over them had been security who had tried to resist in the confusion. That being said men with guns around unarmed civilians tied up, can often deteriorate into men with guns surrounded by dead bodies. As such it was weighing on the commander to try and keep the tense situation under control.

Colonel Dawson stood around his men. Surrounded by communications officers patching him through to the embassy. As he was on the phone with the Ambassador he was directing men, lining them up and getting the unarmed office staff and civil servants onto trucks. It had been a long day. It was noon but the operation began in earnest around 2am a couple hours after the event at London city. He had a cigarette in his mouth. The smell of the bodies had begun to permeate so his first focus was getting the stench of death away and making sure there were not any more corpses to get out of the way. The most difficult part was the residential areas and he still had not figured out what the hell to do about horse guards, the big stronghold. They could not just take it down with an airstrike. It would be too much too violent of an action. Sure, they were doing a coup around London but giving the circumstances and the professionalism he had maintained it could be seen as just stabilizing the frontier. He was trying to get the embassy to go through with some kind of diplomatic solution. He could lay siege to the place too which was the best option once he had wrapped up here. He hung up and took another drag of his cigarette.

As the cat walked up. She was still bleeding. Carrying a haggard rucksack unconsciously. One foot in front of the hour. The Russian uniform caused quite a stir among the American guards. Yet she did not seem armed. As Colonel Dawson was handed a report by the bemused guards he reasoned that she was a survivor from the airport. Whatever information was in that brain might be useful. Thus they were to apprehend her and send her on the next transport to the embassy. She walked towards the walls. Oblivious of the armed guards and its destroyed state. There was nothing running in those circuits besides the single word, forward. Rung around it was fences of pain that blocked much of a deeper thought. She was easy to apprehend. She was already looking for a set of arms to collapse into. Comfort in another human being that she greedily and undeservedly lapped up. An unknown soldier. Anyone would do. The angelic wings of absolution holding around her. She could let go finally.

When she woke up it was as though no time had passed at all. Unlike the unfathomably long dark of before. She knew the flavor on her lips metal and smoke and dirt. The shape too. The barrel of a gun, the muzzle, the compensator. She opened her eyes. Opened her nose. The body she had was still dull and aching. The muscles snapping together still in some places. Maybe some function would not ever return. There was the smell of smoke but not blood. Her hands were tied behind her back and in front of her was Colonel Dawson a black man, toned and tall but not overtly muscular. He had a cigarette in his mouth. A small mountain behind him smoldering away in an ashtray. Next to him was a bespectacled young man with a book open. Russian dictionary field manual, as though he was the poor pitiless private tasked with learning Russian for the Russian camo clad captive now found in their midst.

“It’s okay, I speak English.” Whiskey piped up. Slurring her speech with the gun in her mouth. Speaking came surprisingly naturally back to her. Though there was a slight pain in the left side of her mouth the muscles still locked up.

The private seemed to relax instantly. No, he was not a private a lieutenant marked by the epaulets on his shoulder. American from the uniform.

“You a Rusky? You one of them? Get that barrel out her mouth. I wanna hear what she has to say.” The colonel gestured for the soldiers to stand back. “She’s not a threat come on now, you don’t need to be so happy to go sticking your guns in mouths.”

They pulled back.

“So like I said, are you one of them? You’re wearing their uniform. Holding a Welrod in a half destroyed backpack, here to kill me?” His words were spoken as if daring her to answer yes.

“No. Actually I was sent to try and wipe them out. I got caught up in things and well the rest of my clothes were not terribly up to the journey across from the airport.” She said very matter of factly.

“You expect me to believe that prima facie. Dressed in a Russian uniform. Expect me to believe you were there, you survived the center of it all.” He was rocking back and forth.

“Well yes. I’m not exactly sure how I’m to go about proving it. If you could perhaps tell me where exactly I am right now it would be most appreciated. I was not exactly in the best position to keep track of my location on the journey here.” She tried to gesture to her head but her arms shot in pain and the ties kept her from doing so, as such she made a strange jerking motion.

“She’s reaching for a weapon sir.” One of the overeager privates piped up.

“Quit your yapping private.” The colonel shouted in his direction. Involuntarily Whiskey tensed up. “We’re in the green zone, what’s left. After the impact, if you were there though. Lieutenant Fry, over here.”

The Lieutenant walked over and the two whispered for each other before leaving the command tent.

“I see the green zone. Ah well check through my belongings mister, colonel, was it. You should find my Mutie ID card. If we’re in the green zone then you have my employer that would be a certain DI Mortzavi, portly learned looking fellow with a beard and dark hair.” She was still stumbling on formulating her words but was able to get it across the finish line.

“Private, you are to go find this Detective and bring him here.” Dawson shot a glare in the overeager private’s location.

With his exit it was just Whiskey and the Colonel in the command tent. His cigarette burned to the butt, he smoked it right to the end, and so another was added to his mouth the click of the lighter the only sound in that place. She wanted to talk but did not feel that able to. She felt her consciousness slipping away second by second as though she was to fall back into sleep again. The colonel came towards her and tapped her on the shoulder.

“Hey don’t fall asleep on me we gotta figure this out first.” He spoke softer than he did with the other soldiers.

It brought her out of slumber. There was noise outside the shuffling of feet. Mortzavi’s nosebleed had dried by now but he still had an angry look on his face, which melted away as he looked at her.

“Whiskey, you made it, what are you even doing here. What the hell are you doing? Are you good are you.” He kept on stammering

“We were going to ask you to identify this girl but it looks like you two already know each other, she’s not a Russian asset then.” The Colonel said.

“No, she isn’t. Despite whatever clothes she’s found rummaging around in.” The detective replied.

“Come on now I would make such a good Russian asset.” Whiskey said with a laugh which took a bit of the bite of the pain away.

The detective and colonel both glared at her but with the other soldiers elsewhere they then glared at the private before moving on.

“Detective Mortzavi was it.” The colonel looked over at the detective.

“Yes, it is.”

“Would you testify, what is the name of this girl, that you have knowledge of her, that she is not a Russian asset, and that she is not a danger to the United States.” The colonel said in the sternest voice he had used apart from that which he had used on his direct underlings.

“I Detective Inspector of Reformed New Scotland Yard, do testify that this Mutant, known commonly as Whiskey, is a person with whom I have had substantial correspondence and would be willing to prove that in further questioning with due time for discovery, that she is not to my knowledge a Russian asset and has been cleared by both the Ministry of Justice and MoD, Ministry of Defense serving in various roles and serving her country with distinction, in light of this and her history as part of joint operations with US forces I do not believe that she harbors any ill will towards the United States, nor would she be a danger to them.” He spoke calmly but a bit raggedy he knew the script but populating it with the particular words the colonel needed to hear took more mental bandwidth than he was anticipating.

The colonel looked satisfied. “Okay, I guess we can talk. She served with US forces? Where did she serve? Wait, first you.” Gesturing towards first Whiskey before looking at her cat ears, and then to the stricken detective. “You hold your fingers over your ears, and you, Whiskey, whatever it was, you speak quietly to me. Tell me what campaigns you served in.”

“I was at the tail end of the London campaign and Portsmouth as well as some cleanup operations between Portsmouth and Bristol, I started working, working in the black zone for a couple of years, while also cleaning out whatever people stumbled on in London. Over the past couple of months I’ve just been doing work around the capital, mostly investigating things, any zone related phenomenon, but also a little wet work here and there.” Whiskey said perhaps a bit too honestly though at least she accidentally avoided saying the most potentially incriminating things she could have.

The colonel furrowed his brow. “Okay.” He gestured towards the detective. “Your turn.”

“She served in the London campaign, the liberation of London City airport, the Richmond campaign, as well as cleanup operations in the capital before the wind down of London operations. She served in various roles in Portsmouth and also fighting hard right up and into the Bristol campaign fighting to secure the floating harbor alongside US Marines. It is no understatement to say she owes her life to US support, and several US servicemen owe her their lives. I would not have worked with her, nor would her clearance into the green zone have been cleared otherwise.” This time the detective was more confident.

“Well, I guess I can’t argue with that. You two do know each other. I guess I have one last question, why are you wearing that Russian getup?” The colonel took a slow drag on his cigarette.

“I was at London City, the airport down the river, and I was knocked out after a fight, I woke up in pain with clothes that were unfit to wear, surrounded by dead Russians, and what’s a girl to do. The camouflage isn’t exactly unflattering, I mean it’s no Jietai but it is still rather nice. I still had some facsimile of a backpack but truth be told. I don’t remember thinking about it I am rather in a lot of pain sir. If there would be any way that you could perhaps.” She looked up at both men.

The colonel gave a sigh. “Where are you injured? Wait, you said you were over at London City, the epicenter, and you survived? You were also at the Russian base and survived and somehow you weren’t a double agent?” He started to raise his voice again.

The detective quickly cut in. “I had sent her on a mission to the airport to scout it out actually. If you would allow me to go back to my office I could retrieve the contract and case notes.”

“I guess I can believe that answers one question at least partially.” The colonel looked out for a second and thought about his options. He looked out the tent and shouted “private!”

There were footsteps and then footsteps away. They waited in silence for a minute for her part destroyed backpack. Out of it he took the Welrod. He counted the bullets in the magazine. “Three.” He looked quixotically trying to draw a firm conclusion from scant evidence. He looked at it like a dirty sock found in a kitchen with an empty cookie jar. “Well you fired and shot three times, I guess you were fighting over there.”

“I did just tell you to scout, too much of a glory hound for that are you?” Clark looked over with a smile.

“I’m not a hound. I’m a cat. But that was not in the airport, see I met up with this conscript patrol, instead of shooting me they stood there, one reached for a radio. I shot all three of that patrol dead. I did not use my Welrod in the rest of the fight though, despite intending too. There was enough loud to mask my shots. I use an L1A1 SLR but I think that gun, she didn’t make it.”
